Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is the seller's solicitor's role at the pre-contract stage of a residential conveyancing transaction?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: The seller's solicitor: (1) takes instructions and obtains official copies of the register; (2) investigates title and resolves defects; (3) drafts the contract and sends the contract package to the buyer's solicitor; (4) completes the TA6 (Property Information Form) and TA10 (Fittings and Contents Form); (5) obtains the mortgagee's consent to sale if the property is mortgaged. The seller's solicitor does not conduct searches — that is the buyer's responsibility.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: SELLER'S SOLICITOR · PRE-CONTRACT STAGE · TA6 · TA10 · OFFICIAL COPIES · MORTGAGEE CONSENT

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What documents does the buyer's solicitor typically receive in the contract package, and what is their primary pre-contract task?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: The contract package includes: draft contract, official copies of the register (title register and title plan), TA6, TA10, and any supporting documents (planning permissions, building regs, guarantees). The buyer's solicitor's primary task is to investigate title — verifying the seller has good title free from incumbrances — raise enquiries, conduct searches, and report to the client before exchange.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: BUYER'S SOLICITOR · CONTRACT PACKAGE · TITLE INVESTIGATION · TA6 · TA10 · SEARCHES

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is the legal significance of a seller's answers to pre-contract enquiries (TA6)?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Answers to pre-contract enquiries are contractually significant. A false or misleading answer that the buyer relies on may give rise to a claim in misrepresentation under the Misrepresentation Act 1967. Sellers must update answers if circumstances change before exchange. ⚠️ Answering 'not to my knowledge' does not protect a seller who actually knew of the matter — knowledge is assessed subjectively. The Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 also impose duties on sellers who are traders.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: PRE-CONTRACT ENQUIRIES · TA6 · MISREPRESENTATION ACT 1967 · NOT TO MY KNOWLEDGE · CPUTS 2008

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is the principle of caveat emptor in property transactions and how does it affect the buyer?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Caveat emptor ('buyer beware') means the buyer takes the property as they find it. The seller has no general duty to disclose physical defects or title defects discoverable by inspection, though they must not actively misrepresent. This is why the buyer must: commission a survey, conduct searches, and investigate title independently. The principle has been partially modified by the Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading Regulations 2008 for residential sales where the seller is a trader.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: CAVEAT EMPTOR · BUYER BEWARE · NO DUTY TO DISCLOSE · SURVEY · SEARCHES · CPUTR 2008

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is the legal effect of exchange of contracts in a conveyancing transaction?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Exchange creates a binding contract for the sale of land. After exchange: (1) the buyer acquires an equitable interest in the property; (2) the seller holds the property on constructive trust for the buyer; (3) risk of damage passes to the buyer under the Standard Conditions of Sale (SCS 5th ed.); (4) neither party can withdraw without breach. A 10% deposit is typically paid on exchange. ⚠️ Risk passes to the buyer at exchange — the buyer should insure from that moment. This is a common exam trap.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: EXCHANGE · BINDING CONTRACT · RISK PASSES TO BUYER · EQUITABLE INTEREST · CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST · INSURE FROM EXCHANGE

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What are the three Law Society formulae for exchange of contracts and when is each used?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Formula A — one solicitor holds both signed parts and exchanges; used where one firm holds both parts (rare). Formula B — each solicitor holds their client's signed part; exchange is agreed by telephone, then parts are posted to each other; used in most straightforward transactions. Formula C — a chain synchronisation formula; a solicitor commits to exchange on their purchase only when exchange on their sale is simultaneously confirmed; protects clients in chains. ⚠️ Contracts must comply with s.2 LP(MP)A 1989: in writing, signed by both parties, incorporating all agreed terms.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: FORMULA A · FORMULA B · FORMULA C · CHAIN SYNCHRONISATION · TELEPHONIC EXCHANGE · S.2 LP(MP)A 1989

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What are the requirements for a valid contract for the sale of land under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Under s.2 Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989, a contract for the sale or disposition of an interest in land must: (1) be made in writing; (2) incorporate all expressly agreed terms in one document (or, on exchange, in each part); and (3) be signed by or on behalf of each party. Failure to comply makes the contract void. ⚠️ Oral side agreements are ineffective — all agreed terms must be in the written contract before exchange.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: S.2 LP(MP)A 1989 · IN WRITING · SIGNED · ALL TERMS · VOID IF DEFECTIVE · NO ORAL SIDE AGREEMENTS

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is the deposit in a conveyancing transaction and what happens to it if the buyer defaults?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: The deposit is typically 10% of the purchase price, paid on exchange. It is held by the seller's solicitor as stakeholder (default under SCS — cannot be released until completion) or as agent for the seller (can be used immediately). If the buyer defaults, the seller may forfeit the deposit and sue for any additional loss. If the seller defaults, the buyer may recover the deposit plus interest. ⚠️ If a reduced deposit (e.g. 5%) is agreed, the seller retains the right to forfeit the full 10% — they can sue for the shortfall.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: DEPOSIT · 10% · STAKEHOLDER · AGENT · FORFEITURE · BUYER DEFAULT · REDUCED DEPOSIT SHORTFALL

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What steps must a buyer's solicitor take between exchange and completion?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Between exchange and completion the buyer's solicitor must: (1) raise requisitions on title (if not resolved pre-exchange); (2) approve and engross the transfer deed (TR1); (3) report to the buyer on the mortgage offer and obtain the signed mortgage deed; (4) prepare a completion statement; (5) obtain the mortgage advance from the lender; (6) carry out pre-completion searches — OS1 official search with priority (gives 30 business day protection period) and bankruptcy search (K16) against the buyer; (7) obtain cleared funds from the client.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: BETWEEN EXCHANGE AND COMPLETION · REQUISITIONS · TR1 · OS1 · PRIORITY PERIOD · MORTGAGE ADVANCE

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is an OS1 official search with priority and why is it critical before completion?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: An OS1 is a pre-completion search at HM Land Registry against a registered title. It reveals entries made since the official copies were obtained and grants a 30 business day priority period during which no adverse entry can be registered ahead of the buyer's application. The buyer's solicitor must lodge the registration application within this period. ⚠️ If the priority period expires without registration, any intervening entries (charging orders, second sales) may take priority. The OS1 is against the title number — run a separate K16 bankruptcy search against the seller and buyer personally.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: OS1 OFFICIAL SEARCH · 30 BUSINESS DAYS · PRIORITY PERIOD · K16 BANKRUPTCY SEARCH · LAND REGISTRY

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What happens on completion of a residential conveyancing transaction and when does legal title pass?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: On completion: (1) the balance of the purchase price is transferred to the seller's solicitor via CHAPS; (2) keys are released (usually via the estate agent); (3) the seller's solicitor sends the TR1 and title documents to the buyer's solicitor; (4) the seller's mortgage is redeemed. ⚠️ Legal title (the legal estate) passes on completion — not on exchange. Before completion the buyer holds only an equitable interest. This is a frequently tested distinction.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: COMPLETION · CHAPS · KEYS · LEGAL TITLE PASSES · TR1 · EQUITABLE INTEREST BEFORE COMPLETION

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What are the consequences if a party fails to complete on the contractual completion date under the Standard Conditions of Sale?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Under the SCS 5th ed.: (1) the innocent party may serve a Notice to Complete, making time of the essence; (2) the notice gives 10 working days to complete; (3) if the defaulting party still fails, the innocent party may rescind and claim damages. If the buyer fails: the seller may forfeit the deposit, resell, and sue for the shortfall. If the seller fails: the buyer recovers the deposit with interest and may sue for damages or seek specific performance. ⚠️ Time is not automatically of the essence on the completion date — it becomes so only after a valid Notice to Complete is served.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: FAILURE TO COMPLETE · NOTICE TO COMPLETE · 10 WORKING DAYS · TIME OF THE ESSENCE · SCS · RESCISSION

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What are the buyer's solicitor's post-completion obligations in a residential transaction?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: After completion the buyer's solicitor must: (1) pay SDLT and submit the SDLT1 return to HMRC within 14 days; (2) obtain the SDLT5 certificate from HMRC; (3) register the transfer and new mortgage at HM Land Registry within the OS1 priority period (30 business days); (4) report to client and lender once registration is complete; (5) send updated title information document to the client. ⚠️ The 14-day SDLT deadline is the most urgent post-completion obligation — missing it triggers automatic penalties.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: POST-COMPLETION · SDLT · 14 DAYS · SDLT1 · SDLT5 · LAND REGISTRY · OS1 PRIORITY PERIOD

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is the significance of registration at HM Land Registry and what happens if registration is delayed?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: Under the LRA 2002, registration completes the legal transfer — it is constitutive, not merely declaratory. Until registered, the buyer holds only an equitable interest. If registration is delayed beyond the OS1 priority period (30 business days), intervening entries — a second purchaser's registration, charging order, insolvency entry — may take priority. The buyer's equitable interest may be defeated by a bona fide purchaser of the legal estate for value. Prompt registration is therefore essential for full legal protection.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: REGISTRATION · CONSTITUTIVE · LRA 2002 · EQUITABLE INTEREST UNTIL REGISTERED · PRIORITY PERIOD

Pre-Contract to Registration.front: What is required for first registration of title at HM Land Registry and what is the time limit?
Pre-Contract to Registration.answer: First registration is required when an unregistered legal estate is triggered by a registrable disposition (transfer, grant of mortgage, etc.). Application must be made within 2 months of completion; failure results in the legal estate reverting to the seller as trustee for the buyer. Documents required: FR1 application, conveyance/transfer, evidence of title going back 15 years to a good root, SDLT5 certificate, and fee. ⚠️ The 2-month first registration deadline is distinct from the 30 business day OS1 priority period — do not confuse them.
Pre-Contract to Registration.summary_line: FIRST REGISTRATION · FR1 · 2 MONTHS · GOOD ROOT OF TITLE · 15 YEARS · REVERSION TO SELLER

Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.front: What is the 'subject to contract' qualification and does it prevent a binding contract from arising?
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.answer: 'Subject to contract' is a qualification used in pre-contract correspondence and heads of terms to indicate that no binding agreement is intended until formal contracts are exchanged. It prevents the parties from being contractually bound during negotiations. Under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989, a contract for the sale of land must be in writing, signed, and incorporate all terms — oral agreements and pre-contract correspondence are ineffective. ⚠️ The 'subject to contract' qualification does not, of itself, prevent a binding contract from forming if s.2 requirements are subsequently met — for example, by the parties signing a document containing all terms. Always use 'subject to contract and survey' in early correspondence.
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.summary_line: SUBJECT TO CONTRACT · NOT BINDING · S.2 LP(MP)A 1989 · ALL TERMS IN WRITING · ORAL SIDE AGREEMENT

Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.front: What is gazumping and what protections currently exist against it?
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.answer: Gazumping occurs where the seller accepts a higher offer from a third party after an offer has been accepted but before exchange of contracts. Because there is no binding contract until exchange, gazumping is legal — the seller is free to withdraw. Current protections are limited: (1) there is no statutory protection against gazumping in England and Wales; (2) some sellers offer a 'lock-out agreement' — an agreement not to negotiate with other buyers for a specified period, which is itself binding in contract (Pitt v PHH Asset Management [1994]); (3) buyers should seek early exchange and minimise the pre-exchange period. ⚠️ A lock-out agreement must itself comply with s.2 LP(MP)A 1989 if it constitutes a contract relating to land — though courts have held that a promise not to negotiate is not itself a land contract.
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.summary_line: GAZUMPING · LEGAL · NO BINDING CONTRACT BEFORE EXCHANGE · LOCK-OUT AGREEMENT · PITT v PHH · DAMAGES

Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.front: What is the difference between a conveyance, a transfer, and an assent in property transactions?
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.answer: These are the three main documents used to transfer ownership of land: (1) Conveyance — a deed used to transfer unregistered freehold land; (2) Transfer (TR1) — a Land Registry form used to transfer registered freehold or leasehold land; it must be executed as a deed and registered at HM Land Registry to transfer the legal estate; (3) Assent — a document used by personal representatives (executors/administrators) to vest a deceased person's property in a beneficiary; must be in writing and signed by the PRs. ⚠️ In registered land, the TR1 is used — not a conveyance. The TR1 must be registered to transfer the legal title.
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.summary_line: CONVEYANCE · TRANSFER TR1 · ASSENT · UNREGISTERED vs REGISTERED · DEED · REGISTRATION REQUIRED

Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.front: What are the key requirements for a valid deed in a conveyancing transaction?
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.answer: Under s.1 LP(MP)A 1989, a valid deed must: (1) make clear on its face that it is intended to be a deed (by using the word 'deed' or similar); (2) be validly executed — for an individual: signed in the presence of a witness who attests the signature; for a company: either signed by two authorised signatories or signed by one director in the presence of a witness; (3) be delivered — the grantor must intend to be bound immediately. A document that lacks any of these elements is not a deed and cannot transfer a legal estate or create a legal mortgage. ⚠️ An electronic signature can constitute a valid signature on a deed if the formalities for witnessing are met — but electronic witnessing remains restricted.
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.summary_line: VALID DEED · S.1 LP(MP)A 1989 · DEED · SIGNED · WITNESSED · DELIVERED · COMPANY EXECUTION

Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.front: What is the 'seller's duty of disclosure' in a conveyancing transaction and what are its limits?
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.answer: The seller has a limited duty of disclosure. They must disclose: (1) latent defects in title — incumbrances that would not be apparent on inspection (e.g. an undisclosed restrictive covenant); they need not disclose patent defects (those apparent on inspection). They must not actively misrepresent any matter. They do not need to disclose: physical defects (caveat emptor applies); matters that would be revealed by the buyer's own searches or survey. ⚠️ The distinction between latent and patent defects is fact-specific. A seller who knowingly fails to disclose a latent title defect may be liable in misrepresentation — and may be in breach of the contract if the title cannot be made good.
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.summary_line: SELLER DUTY OF DISCLOSURE · LATENT DEFECTS · PATENT DEFECTS · CAVEAT EMPTOR · MISREPRESENTATION

Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.front: What is a 'lock-out agreement' in property transactions and what are the requirements for it to be binding?
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.answer: A lock-out agreement is an agreement by a seller not to negotiate with other prospective buyers for a specified period. It is a negative covenant — a promise not to do something — and does not oblige the seller to sell. In Pitt v PHH Asset Management [1994] CA, the Court of Appeal held that a lock-out agreement could be contractually binding provided: (1) it is supported by consideration; (2) it specifies the period during which the seller will not negotiate with others. A lock-out agreement is not a contract for the sale of land and so need not comply with s.2 LP(MP)A 1989 (which governs contracts to sell, not agreements not to sell). ⚠️ A lock-out agreement without a specified time period is void for uncertainty. If the seller breaches it, the buyer's remedy is damages — not specific performance.
Pre-Contract \u2014 Subject to Contract.summary_line: LOCK-OUT AGREEMENT · PITT v PHH · BINDING · CONSIDERATION · TIME PERIOD · DAMAGES NOT SP

Transaction Stages \u2014 Framework Card.front: Set out the key stages of a residential conveyancing transaction in sequence.
Transaction Stages \u2014 Framework Card.answer: The key stages in order: (1) Instructions and funding confirmed; (2) Contract package received (seller's solicitor sends draft contract, official copies, TA6, TA10); (3) Buyer's solicitor investigates title, raises enquiries, conducts searches; (4) Mortgage offer received and reported to client; (5) Report to client — title, searches, mortgage, contract; (6) Exchange of contracts — binding contract formed, deposit paid, completion date fixed; (7) Pre-completion steps — OS1 search, TR1 engrossed, completion statement, funds obtained; (8) Completion — balance transferred, keys released, legal title passes; (9) Post-completion — SDLT paid within 14 days, registration at Land Registry within OS1 priority period. ⚠️ Legal title passes at completion (step 8), not exchange (step 6). Risk passes to buyer at exchange.
Transaction Stages \u2014 Framework Card.summary_line: CONVEYANCING STAGES · INSTRUCTIONS · EXCHANGE · COMPLETION · SDLT 14 DAYS · REGISTRATION OS1 PERIOD

Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.front: Pitt v PHH Asset Management Ltd [1994]
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.issue: Whether an informal agreement by a seller not to consider other offers constituted a binding lock-out agreement.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.rule: A lock-out agreement — a promise not to negotiate with third parties for a specified period — is a valid and binding contract provided it is supported by consideration and specifies the period of exclusivity. It is not a contract for the sale of land and need not comply with s.2 LP(MP)A 1989.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.application: PHH agreed not to consider other offers for two weeks while Pitt's solicitors prepared the contract. PHH then accepted a higher offer within that period. The Court of Appeal held the lock-out agreement was binding and PHH was in breach.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.conclusion: Lock-out agreements are enforceable in contract. They must specify a time period — an open-ended lock-out is void for uncertainty. The remedy for breach is damages, not specific performance. Practical significance: lock-out agreements give buyers short-term protection against gazumping without creating a binding sale contract.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.summary_line: PITT v PHH · LOCK-OUT AGREEMENT · BINDING · CONSIDERATION · TIME PERIOD · DAMAGES ONLY

Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.issue: Whether a specifically enforceable agreement for a lease gave the tenant the same rights as if a formal lease had been granted.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.rule: Equity treats as done that which ought to be done. Where there is a specifically enforceable contract to create a legal interest in land, equity will treat the parties as if the legal interest had already been granted — the tenant holds an equitable lease on the same terms as the agreed legal lease.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.application: Lonsdale agreed to grant a 7-year lease at a rent payable in advance. No deed was executed. Walsh went into possession and paid rent in arrears (as under a yearly tenancy at common law). When Lonsdale distrained for rent in advance, Walsh argued he held only a legal yearly tenancy. Jessel MR held the equitable lease prevailed.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.conclusion: An agreement for a lease, if specifically enforceable, creates an equitable lease equivalent to the intended legal lease. Relevance in conveyancing: between exchange and completion, the buyer holds an equitable interest under the contract — registration is still needed to obtain full legal title (LRA 2002). An equitable lease is weaker than a legal lease — it is not binding on a bona fide purchaser of the legal estate for value without notice.
Key Cases \u2014 Formation & Equitable L.summary_line: WALSH v LONSDALE · EQUITABLE LEASE · SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · EQUITY TREATS AS DONE · NOT PROPRIETARY

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Contracts for the sale of 14 Maple Avenue are exchanged on 1 March. Completion is fixed for 29 March. On 15 March a burst pipe causes significant water damage to the kitchen. The seller has buildings insurance but the buyer does not. Who bears the risk of the damage and why?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: The buyer bears the risk. Under the Standard Conditions of Sale (5th ed.) condition 5.1, risk passes to the buyer on exchange of contracts — not on completion. The damage occurred after exchange (1 March) but before completion (29 March), so the loss falls on the buyer even though legal title has not yet passed. The buyer should have arranged buildings insurance from exchange. The seller's insurance is irrelevant once risk has passed. Practical rule: advise buyer clients to insure from the moment of exchange.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: RISK AT EXCHANGE · SCS CONDITION 5.1 · BUYER INSURES FROM EXCHANGE · NOT COMPLETION · LEGAL TITLE SEPARATE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Paula is selling her flat. She exchanges contracts on a Monday using Formula B. The following Wednesday, her solicitor realises they posted Paula's signed part to the buyer's solicitor but forgot to date the contract. The buyer's solicitor has already sent their client's signed part. Is there a valid contract?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: Yes, a valid contract has been formed. Under Formula B, exchange occurs at the moment both solicitors agree on the telephone that exchange has taken place — the physical posting of the parts follows the telephonic exchange. The contract is binding from the moment of that agreement, provided both parts were signed and incorporated all agreed terms (s.2 LP(MP)A 1989). An undated contract is not void — the date of exchange can be established by reference to the telephonic agreement and contemporaneous evidence. The missing date is a clerical error, not a failure to comply with s.2.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: FORMULA B · TELEPHONIC EXCHANGE · BINDING ON PHONE · UNDATED CONTRACT NOT VOID · S.2 SATISFIED

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: On exchange, Carl pays a 5% deposit (£15,000 on a £300,000 purchase) rather than the standard 10%, with the seller's agreement. Carl subsequently defaults and fails to complete. What can the seller recover?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: The seller may forfeit the £15,000 deposit paid and sue Carl for the remaining £15,000 (to make up the full 10% the seller is entitled to forfeit). The seller's right to forfeit 10% of the purchase price is preserved under the Standard Conditions of Sale even where a reduced deposit was agreed — the seller can sue for the shortfall between the reduced deposit and 10%. In addition, if the seller suffers further loss on resale (e.g. the property is sold at a lower price), the seller can claim damages for that additional loss. The buyer cannot argue that forfeiture of only 5% was impliedly agreed.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: REDUCED DEPOSIT · 5% · SELLER FORFEITS PAID + SUES FOR SHORTFALL TO 10% · SCS · ADDITIONAL DAMAGES

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Completion of Helen's purchase is due on Friday. By 2pm the seller's solicitor has not received the completion funds. Helen's solicitor failed to transfer the mortgage advance from the lender in time. Can the seller rescind immediately?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: No. Under the Standard Conditions of Sale, time is not of the essence for the completion date. The seller cannot rescind immediately for failure to complete on the due date. The seller's remedy is to serve a Notice to Complete, which makes time of the essence and gives the buyer 10 working days to complete. Only if the buyer fails to complete within that 10-day period can the seller rescind, forfeit the deposit, and sue for further loss. The seller should not re-list the property or purport to rescind without first serving the notice — doing so would itself be a repudiatory breach.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: NOTICE TO COMPLETE · TIME NOT OF ESSENCE AUTOMATICALLY · 10 WORKING DAYS · SCS · RESCIND AFTER NOTICE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Marcus completes the purchase of a registered freehold property on 10 April. His solicitor submits the SDLT return and pays the SDLT on 30 April. The registration application is lodged on 2 May (within the OS1 priority period). Has Marcus's solicitor met all post-completion deadlines?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: The SDLT deadline has been missed. SDLT must be paid and the SDLT1 return submitted within 14 days of the effective date (completion on 10 April) — the deadline was therefore 24 April. Submitting on 30 April is 6 days late. This will trigger an automatic penalty (£100 for up to 3 months late) and interest on the overdue tax. The registration lodged on 2 May within the OS1 priority period is fine provided it is within 30 business days of the OS1 search. The solicitor must advise Marcus of the late filing and the penalties incurred.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: SDLT 14-DAY DEADLINE · LATE FILING · AUTOMATIC PENALTY · INTEREST · OS1 PRIORITY PERIOD SEPARATE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Diane is buying 22 Oak Street from a company. The official copies show a restriction on the proprietorship register: 'No disposition of the registered estate by the proprietor of the registered estate is to be registered without a written consent signed by the proprietor of the charge dated [date] in favour of Barclays Bank plc.' What must Diane's solicitor do before the transfer can be registered?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: Diane's solicitor must obtain Barclays Bank's written consent to the disposition before the transfer can be registered. The restriction indicates that the property is subject to a registered legal charge in favour of Barclays. In practice, this means either: (1) the seller must redeem the mortgage from the completion monies and Barclays will issue a Form DS1 or authorise removal via e-DS1, enabling the restriction to be cancelled; or (2) Barclays must provide formal written consent to the sale. In a standard conveyancing transaction, the seller's solicitor requests the redemption figure, redeems the mortgage on completion, and the buyer's solicitor lodges the DS1 with the registration application. The restriction cannot be ignored — Land Registry will not register the transfer without it being complied with.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: LENDER'S RESTRICTION · DS1 · E-DS1 · REDEMPTION · LAND REGISTRY WON'T REGISTER WITHOUT COMPLIANCE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Sophie exchanges contracts to sell her home on 3 June. She then receives a much higher offer from another buyer and wants to withdraw. Can she do so?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: No. Once contracts are exchanged, a binding contract for the sale of land exists and neither party can withdraw without being in breach. Sophie's attempt to pull out would be a repudiatory breach, entitling the buyer to: (1) sue for damages (including loss of bargain and wasted costs); (2) seek specific performance of the contract (a court order requiring Sophie to complete the sale at the agreed price). Sophie cannot lawfully accept the higher offer. This is why gazumping (accepting a higher offer before exchange) is legal, but withdrawing after exchange is not.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: AFTER EXCHANGE · BINDING CONTRACT · CANNOT WITHDRAW · SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE · DAMAGES · SELLER BREACH

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Ben's solicitor carries out an OS1 official search on 5 May. Completion takes place on 6 May. Ben's solicitor lodges the registration application on 20 July. Is Ben's title fully protected?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: No — Ben's title is at risk. The OS1 priority period is 30 business days from the date of the search (5 May). 30 business days from 5 May (excluding weekends and bank holidays) expires well before 20 July. By lodging the registration application after the priority period expired, Ben's solicitor lost the protection. Any entries made at Land Registry between the expiry of the priority period and the lodging of the application (e.g. a charging order against a previous owner, a second transfer) may take priority over Ben's registration. Ben holds only an equitable interest in the interim and may be exposed to competing claims. The solicitor may have committed a negligent act.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: OS1 PRIORITY PERIOD · 30 BUSINESS DAYS · LATE REGISTRATION · PRIORITY LOST · EQUITABLE INTEREST ONLY

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: Jo is buying an unregistered freehold property. The seller produces a chain of title going back 18 years to a conveyance on sale. The buyer's solicitor notices that 12 years ago, the property was transferred by way of a gift between siblings. Should this cause concern?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: Yes — the voluntary conveyance (gift) should be investigated. A voluntary conveyance is not a good root of title. However, in this scenario the root is the conveyance on sale 18 years ago, which predates the gift — the root is sound (18 years old, on sale, describing the property and dealing with the whole interest). The concern is different: a voluntary conveyance may be vulnerable to challenge under the Insolvency Act 1986 (transaction at an undervalue) if the donor was insolvent or became insolvent within a prescribed period. The buyer's solicitor should check whether insolvency searches reveal any issues against the donor at the time of the gift. Indemnity insurance may be advisable if no clean insolvency picture can be established.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: VOLUNTARY CONVEYANCE NOT GOOD ROOT · ROOT IS CONVEYANCE ON SALE · IA 1986 INSOLVENCY RISK · INSURANCE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.front: A seller and buyer agree in a telephone call that the buyer can have the garden shed as part of the sale, but this is not included in the contract or the TA10. After exchange, the seller removes the shed. Can the buyer enforce the oral agreement?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.answer: No. Under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989, a contract for the sale of an interest in land must incorporate all agreed terms in the written contract. The oral agreement about the shed was not incorporated in the written contract — it is therefore unenforceable as a contractual term. The buyer cannot rely on the side agreement. If the shed is a fixture (attached to the land and part of the property), it passes with the property automatically — the seller would be in breach by removing it. If the shed is a chattel (not a fixture — freestanding, removable), the seller is entitled to take it unless it was included in the TA10. The buyer's remedy, if any, would be in misrepresentation if the seller made a false pre-contract representation about the shed's inclusion.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Residential.summary_line: ORAL AGREEMENT NOT BINDING · S.2 LP(MP)A 1989 · ALL TERMS IN WRITING · FIXTURE vs CHATTEL · TA10

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: What are heads of terms in a commercial property transaction and what is their legal status?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: Heads of terms record the principal agreed terms (price, property, conditions) at the start of a commercial transaction. They are almost always expressed 'subject to contract' and are therefore not legally binding. ⚠️ If heads are signed without a 'subject to contract' qualification and contain all essential terms, there is a risk they constitute a binding contract under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989. Always mark commercial heads 'subject to contract and survey'.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: HEADS OF TERMS · SUBJECT TO CONTRACT · NOT BINDING · S.2 LP(MP)A 1989 · COMMERCIAL PROPERTY

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: How does a commercial conveyancing transaction typically differ from a residential one?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: Key differences: (1) VAT is often payable on commercial property — option to tax must be checked; (2) SDLT applies at commercial rates; (3) The Standard Commercial Property Conditions (SCPC) are used instead of SCS; (4) Due diligence is more extensive — environmental, planning, tenant checks; (5) Security of tenure may arise under the LTA 1954 for commercial tenants; (6) Transactions commonly involve lease assignment or grant rather than freehold sale; (7) No standard timetable — the process is negotiated between the parties.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: COMMERCIAL CONVEYANCING · VAT · SDLT COMMERCIAL RATES · SCPC · LTA 1954 · DUE DILIGENCE

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: What is the VAT position on commercial property and what is the option to tax?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: Commercial property is normally VAT-exempt. A seller or landlord may elect to waive exemption — 'opting to tax' — making their supplies standard-rated (20%). Once opted: (1) VAT is charged on sale price or rent; (2) input VAT on costs is recoverable. The option must be notified to HMRC within 30 days and is irrevocable for 20 years. ⚠️ A buyer who is not VAT-registered cannot recover the VAT — significantly increasing acquisition cost. Always check for an existing option to tax before exchange.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: OPTION TO TAX · VAT-EXEMPT DEFAULT · STANDARD-RATED · 20% · 30 DAYS HMRC · 20 YEAR IRREVOCABLE

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: When does the Transfer of a Going Concern (TOGC) exemption apply and why does it matter?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: TOGC removes a commercial property sale from the scope of VAT entirely even where the seller has opted to tax. Conditions: (1) the property is sold with a sitting tenant (income-producing at completion); (2) the buyer opts to tax before completion; (3) the buyer notifies HMRC of the option before completion. TOGC saves the buyer from paying VAT upfront and then recovering it — a significant cashflow benefit. ⚠️ If the buyer fails to opt to tax before completion, TOGC fails and the seller must charge 20% VAT.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: TOGC · SITTING TENANT · BUYER MUST OPT TO TAX · HMRC NOTIFICATION · BEFORE COMPLETION · CASHFLOW

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: What are the principal terms typically negotiated in a commercial lease?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: Key commercial lease terms: (1) Rent — amount and review mechanism (commonly upward-only open market review every 5 years); (2) Term — length and break clauses; (3) Permitted use — restricted use covenant; (4) Repair — FRI (full repairing and insuring) leases are standard; (5) Alienation — assignment and subletting rights, usually requiring landlord's consent (not to be unreasonably withheld); (6) Service charge — in multi-let buildings; (7) Alterations — tenant's right to carry out works and reinstatement obligations on expiry.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: COMMERCIAL LEASE · RENT · FRI · BREAK CLAUSE · ALIENATION · SERVICE CHARGE · ALTERATIONS

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: What security of tenure does a commercial tenant have under the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: Under LTA 1954 Pt II, a commercial tenancy continues after the contractual end date until terminated by the correct statutory procedure, and the tenant has the right to apply to court for a new tenancy on similar terms. The landlord can only oppose renewal on statutory grounds (s.30), including: (a) persistent rent arrears; (b) substantial breach of covenants; (f) intention to demolish or reconstruct; (g) intention to occupy personally. ⚠️ The parties can contract out of the Act using the warning notice and declaration procedure (post-2004). Contracting out removes the tenant's renewal right entirely — advise tenant clients of this before signing.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: LTA 1954 · SECURITY OF TENURE · S.30 GROUNDS · CONTRACTING OUT · WARNING NOTICE · DECLARATION

Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.front: What is a break clause in a commercial lease and what conditions typically apply?
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.answer: A break clause allows one or both parties to terminate the lease early on notice. Break clauses may be tenant-only, landlord-only, or mutual. Conditions typically attached to a tenant's break: (a) rent must be fully paid; (b) vacant possession must be given; (c) all material covenants must be complied with. ⚠️ Courts have strictly construed break conditions — even a minor rent arrear or a tenant's fitting left at the property can invalidate the break notice. Advise tenant clients to comply meticulously with all conditions.
Commercial Conveyancing & Leases.summary_line: BREAK CLAUSE · CONDITIONS · RENT TO DATE · VACANT POSSESSION · STRICT CONSTRUCTION · INVALIDITY

LTA 1954 \u2014 Contracting Out.front: What is the difference between contracting out and not contracting out of the LTA 1954 and what procedure is required?
LTA 1954 \u2014 Contracting Out.answer: LTA 1954 Pt II gives commercial tenants the right to remain in the property after the lease ends and apply for a new tenancy. Contracting out removes this right entirely. Procedure (post-2004 Regulatory Reform Order): (1) before the lease is granted, the landlord serves a statutory warning notice on the tenant; (2) the tenant (or their solicitor) makes a statutory declaration that they have received and understood the notice; (3) the warning notice and declaration must be completed before the lease is granted; (4) the lease must contain a reference to the contracting-out agreement. ⚠️ The procedure is strict — if the warning notice is served fewer than 14 days before the lease is granted, the tenant must make a statutory declaration by deed (not simply a simple declaration). Failure to follow the procedure means the lease retains full security of tenure.
LTA 1954 \u2014 Contracting Out.summary_line: LTA 1954 CONTRACTING OUT · WARNING NOTICE · STATUTORY DECLARATION · BEFORE GRANT · 14 DAYS DEED

Key Cases \u2014 Service Charges.issue: Whether a landlord could recover service charges that included costs not expressly mentioned in the service charge provisions of the lease.
Key Cases \u2014 Service Charges.rule: A landlord can only recover service charges that fall within the service charge clause in the lease — interpreted strictly. The costs must be ones that the lease expressly or by necessary implication authorises. Service charges must also be reasonably incurred and the works/services must be of a reasonable standard (LTA 1985 s.19).
Key Cases \u2014 Service Charges.application: The landlord sought to recover as service charge items costs that were not clearly authorised by the service charge provisions in the lease. The Court of Appeal held that service charge clauses are construed strictly — ambiguities are resolved against the landlord (the party who drafted the clause). The disputed costs could not be recovered.
Key Cases \u2014 Service Charges.conclusion: Service charge clauses are construed strictly. A landlord who wishes to recover a type of cost must ensure it is expressly included in the lease. The tenant can challenge service charges at the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) under LTA 1985. ⚠️ The statutory reasonableness requirement (s.19 LTA 1985) is a floor — the landlord must also stay within the scope of the lease clause. Both limits apply cumulatively.
Key Cases \u2014 Service Charges.summary_line: GOLDMILE v LECHOURITIS · SERVICE CHARGES · STRICT CONSTRUCTION · LANDLORD CANNOT RECOVER OUTSIDE CLAUSE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.front: LandCo Ltd is selling a commercial office building for £2m. LandCo opted to tax the property five years ago. The buyer, RetailCo Ltd, is not VAT registered. What is the VAT position and what is the practical impact on RetailCo?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.answer: Because LandCo has opted to tax, the sale is standard-rated and LandCo must charge 20% VAT on the £2m purchase price — an additional £400,000. RetailCo, not being VAT registered, cannot recover the input VAT. RetailCo therefore pays £2.4m in total, with the £400,000 VAT being an irrecoverable cost. RetailCo's solicitor should have identified the option to tax before exchange and negotiated accordingly — either seeking a price reduction, seeking to become VAT registered before completion, or exploring whether the TOGC exemption applies. TOGC would not apply here unless RetailCo becomes VAT registered and opts to tax before completion and there is a sitting tenant. This is a significant commercial issue that should have been flagged in pre-contract due diligence.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.summary_line: OPTION TO TAX · VAT 20% · UNREGISTERED BUYER CANNOT RECOVER · DUE DILIGENCE · PRE-CONTRACT CHECK

Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.front: PropCo is selling a commercial investment property with a sitting tenant to InvestCo. PropCo has opted to tax. InvestCo opts to tax and notifies HMRC the day before completion. Does TOGC apply?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.answer: Yes — TOGC applies. The conditions are met: (1) the property is sold with a sitting tenant, making it a going concern (income-producing business); (2) InvestCo has opted to tax before completion; (3) InvestCo has notified HMRC of the option before completion. Provided HMRC's notification requirements are satisfied, the sale falls outside the scope of VAT — PropCo does not charge VAT on the sale price despite having opted to tax. This avoids InvestCo paying £20% VAT upfront and then recovering it. The timing of the option is critical — opting to tax after completion would cause TOGC to fail and VAT would be chargeable.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.summary_line: TOGC · SITTING TENANT · BUYER OPTS TO TAX BEFORE COMPLETION · HMRC NOTIFICATION · NO VAT

Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.front: TechStart Ltd takes a 10-year commercial lease of office space. The parties sign heads of terms but do not carry out the LTA 1954 contracting-out procedure. The lease is then granted. At the end of the term, TechStart wants to stay. Can the landlord refuse to renew?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.answer: TechStart has security of tenure under the LTA 1954 Pt II because the contracting-out procedure was not completed before the lease was granted. The landlord cannot simply refuse to renew — TechStart's tenancy will continue after the contractual end date and TechStart can apply to the court for a new tenancy on similar terms. The landlord can only oppose renewal on the statutory grounds in s.30 LTA 1954 (e.g. intention to demolish/reconstruct under ground (f), or intention to occupy personally under ground (g)). If no ground applies, the court must order a new tenancy. The landlord's solicitor should have ensured the warning notice and statutory declaration procedure was completed before the lease was executed.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.summary_line: LTA 1954 · SECURITY OF TENURE · NO CONTRACTING OUT · TENANT RENEWAL RIGHT · S.30 GROUNDS

Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.front: A commercial tenant holds a 15-year lease with a break clause exercisable at year 10, provided 6 months' written notice is given and the rent is paid to date. The tenant serves the break notice with rent £200 in arrears. Is the break effective?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.answer: No — the break is not effective. Break clause conditions are construed strictly. The requirement that rent be paid to date is a condition precedent to the exercise of the break. Even a small arrear (here, £200) means the condition is not satisfied and the break notice is ineffective. The lease continues to year 15. This is confirmed by the strict approach courts have taken to break conditions following cases such as Riverside Park Ltd v NHS Property Services Ltd [2016]. The tenant should have ensured all rent was paid before serving the notice — even a minor underpayment can invalidate the break. The tenant's solicitor should audit the rent account carefully before advising that the break has been validly exercised.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.summary_line: BREAK CLAUSE · STRICT CONDITIONS · MINOR ARREAR · BREAK FAILS · LEASE CONTINUES · AUDIT RENT ACCOUNT

Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.front: A commercial landlord becomes aware that the tenant has sublet part of the premises without consent, in breach of the alienation covenant. The landlord wants to forfeit. However, last month the landlord accepted the quarterly rent payment knowing of the breach. Can the landlord now forfeit?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.answer: No — the landlord has waived the right to forfeit for this breach. Waiver occurs when a landlord, with knowledge of the breach, does an unequivocal act recognising the continued existence of the tenancy. Accepting rent after becoming aware of the breach is the classic example of waiver. By accepting last month's payment, the landlord elected to treat the tenancy as continuing and cannot now forfeit for that specific breach of the subletting covenant. The landlord's options are: (1) seek an injunction requiring the sub-tenant to vacate; (2) claim damages; (3) wait for a fresh breach and ensure they do not waive again. ⚠️ Waiver is automatic — it does not require intention. Even a demand for rent (not just receipt) can constitute waiver.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Commercial.summary_line: WAIVER · KNOWLEDGE OF BREACH · ACCEPTANCE OF RENT · AUTOMATIC · CANNOT FORFEIT THIS BREACH

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: What are the three registers that make up a registered title at HM Land Registry?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: A registered title has three registers: (1) Property Register — describes the property, its tenure (freehold/leasehold), and rights benefiting the land (easements, covenants in favour); (2) Proprietorship Register — states the class of title, the registered owner's name and address, and any restrictions on dealing; (3) Charges Register — records incumbrances burdening the land: mortgages, restrictive covenants, easements granted, and other registered charges. The official copies of all three are obtained on form OC1 and form the starting point for title investigation.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: THREE REGISTERS · PROPERTY REGISTER · PROPRIETORSHIP REGISTER · CHARGES REGISTER · OC1 · TITLE INVESTIGATION

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: What are the classes of registered title and which is the strongest?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: Classes of registered title: (1) Absolute title — the best class; the owner's title is guaranteed by the state subject only to overriding interests and entries on the register. Confers ownership free from any prior claims. (2) Qualified title — title is guaranteed subject to a specified defect. Rare. (3) Possessory title — typically granted where the applicant cannot produce documentary evidence of title (e.g. squatter's title). Subject to any estate or interest prior to registration. (4) Good leasehold title — freehold title has not been examined; the leasehold title is good but the landlord's title cannot be guaranteed. ⚠️ Absolute title is the gold standard — all other classes carry risk and should be investigated further.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: ABSOLUTE TITLE · QUALIFIED TITLE · POSSESSORY TITLE · GOOD LEASEHOLD · CLASSES OF TITLE · LRA 2002

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: What is a restriction on a registered title and when is one commonly encountered?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: A restriction is an entry on the proprietorship register limiting the circumstances in which a registered disposition can be made without some further step. Common examples: (1) a trust restriction — 'No disposition by a sole proprietor of the registered estate... unless authorised by an order of the court' — indicating the land is held on trust and capital money must be paid to at least two trustees; (2) a charge restriction — requiring the lender's consent to a sale; (3) a management company restriction requiring a certificate of compliance with lease covenants. ⚠️ A buyer must comply with any restriction before the transfer can be registered — failure to do so means the transfer is void for registration purposes.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: RESTRICTION · PROPRIETORSHIP REGISTER · CONDITION ON DISPOSITION · TRUST · LENDER CONSENT · COMPLIANCE

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: What are overriding interests under the LRA 2002 and why are they significant for buyers?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: Overriding interests (LRA 2002 Schs 1 and 3) bind a registered proprietor even though they do not appear on the register. They are significant because a buyer who purchases registered land takes subject to them without notice. Key Schedule 3 overriding interests on a registered disposition include: (1) legal leases not exceeding 7 years (Sch 3 para 1); (2) interests of persons in actual occupation, where the interest would have been apparent on a reasonable inspection or the occupier disclosed it on enquiry (Sch 3 para 2); (3) legal easements and profits acquired by implied grant or prescription (Sch 3 para 3). ⚠️ Actual occupation is the highest-risk overriding interest in practice — always make enquiries of occupiers and inspect the property.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: OVERRIDING INTERESTS · SCH 1 · SCH 3 · LRA 2002 · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · LEGAL LEASES · EASEMENTS

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: When does a person in actual occupation have an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: A person in actual occupation has an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002 where: (1) they have an interest in registered land; (2) they are in actual occupation at the time of the disposition; and (3) either: (a) the interest would have been apparent on a reasonably careful inspection of the land at the time of the disposition, or (b) the occupier disclosed the interest on reasonable enquiry. ⚠️ The buyer loses protection from the overriding interest if they either fail to inspect or fail to enquire of occupiers. Physical inspection and occupier enquiries are essential pre-contract steps.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: SCH 3 PARA 2 · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · APPARENT ON INSPECTION · ENQUIRY · INSPECT PROPERTY · OCCUPIERS

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: How does a seller deduce title in an unregistered land transaction?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: In unregistered land, the seller deduces title by providing an epitome of title — a schedule of all relevant title documents going back at least 15 years to a good root of title (s.44 LPA 1925). The root of title must: (1) be at least 15 years old; (2) deal with the whole legal and equitable interest; (3) describe the property sufficiently; and (4) contain nothing to cast doubt on title. A conveyance or a mortgage by demise are good roots; a voluntary conveyance or an assent is not a good root. The buyer then investigates the chain of ownership from the root to the present day.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: UNREGISTERED TITLE · EPITOME OF TITLE · GOOD ROOT · S.44 LPA 1925 · 15 YEARS · CONVEYANCE ON SALE

Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.subtopic: Title, Overriding Interests & Third Party Rights
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.front: How are third party interests protected in unregistered land?
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.answer: In unregistered land, protection depends on the nature of the interest: (1) Legal interests — binding on the whole world (e.g. legal mortgages, legal easements); (2) Registrable land charges — protected by registration at the Land Charges Register under the LCA 1972 (e.g. restrictive covenants post-1925, estate contracts, equitable easements). Registration constitutes actual notice; non-registration means the charge is void against a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate; (3) Overriding interests in unregistered land — binding on the purchaser regardless (e.g. legal leases not exceeding 21 years, legal easements); (4) Other equitable interests — governed by the doctrine of notice (bona fide purchaser of the legal estate for value without notice takes free). ⚠️ The land charges register is searched against names, not property — a conveyancing defect can arise if the search is against the wrong name.
Title, Overriding Interests & Third Part.summary_line: UNREGISTERED LAND · LAND CHARGES · LCA 1972 · LEGAL INTERESTS · DOCTRINE OF NOTICE · REGISTRATION

Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.front: What is an overriding interest under Schedule 1 LRA 2002 (on first registration) vs Schedule 3 (on a registered disposition)?
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.answer: Schedules 1 and 3 LRA 2002 both list overriding interests but apply at different stages: Schedule 1 applies on first registration — these interests bind the registered proprietor from the moment of first registration, even without notice. Schedule 3 applies on a registered disposition — these interests bind a disponee (e.g. a buyer) who takes a registered title. The lists are broadly similar but Sch 3 is slightly narrower for some categories. Key Sch 3 overriding interests: (1) legal leases ≤7 years; (2) interests of persons in actual occupation (with qualification); (3) legal easements where use was apparent or recent or where the disponee knew of the easement. ⚠️ The qualification for actual occupation under Sch 3 (but not Sch 1) means that an occupier who fails to disclose their interest when asked does not have an overriding interest under Sch 3.
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.summary_line: SCH 1 v SCH 3 · LRA 2002 · FIRST REGISTRATION · REGISTERED DISPOSITION · DISCLOSURE QUALIFICATION

Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.front: What is 'indemnity insurance' in conveyancing and in what circumstances is it used?
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.answer: Indemnity insurance is a policy taken out to protect the insured against a specific identified risk — a defect in title or a potential liability — that cannot be resolved by conventional means. Common uses: (1) missing planning permission or building regulations consent; (2) restrictive covenant breach (where the beneficiary cannot be traced); (3) chancel repair liability; (4) lack of evidence of a right (e.g. an easement used but not expressly granted); (5) insolvency transactions — risk of transaction being set aside. The policy compensates the insured for loss suffered as a result of the risk materialising. ⚠️ Indemnity insurance is a risk management tool, not a cure — it does not remove the defect. The buyer must disclose the existence of the policy to the insurer if the risk is later enforced. Contacting the potential claimant will void most policies.
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.summary_line: INDEMNITY INSURANCE · TITLE DEFECT · RISK MANAGEMENT · NOT A CURE · DO NOT CONTACT CLAIMANT

Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.front: What is a 'good root of title' in unregistered conveyancing and what are its requirements?
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.answer: A good root of title must be: (1) at least 15 years old at the date of the contract (s.44 LPA 1925); (2) a document dealing with the whole legal and equitable interest in the property; (3) describing the property sufficiently; (4) containing nothing to cast doubt on the title. Examples of good roots: a conveyance on sale; a legal mortgage by demise; a vesting deed. Examples that are NOT good roots: a voluntary conveyance (gift — raises questions of insolvency); an assent; an equitable mortgage. ⚠️ The 15-year period starts from the date of the root document to the date of contract — not completion.
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.summary_line: GOOD ROOT OF TITLE · 15 YEARS · CONVEYANCE ON SALE · WHOLE INTEREST · NOT VOLUNTARY CONVEYANCE

Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.front: What is a 'requisition on title' and at what stage is it raised?
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.answer: A requisition on title is a formal question raised by the buyer's solicitor to the seller's solicitor about a matter arising from the title or the draft contract. Requisitions are typically raised after receipt of the contract package and must be raised before exchange. Standard requisitions include: confirmation of vacant possession on completion, details of the seller's mortgage (redemption statement), evidence of authority to sell (e.g. if the seller is a company or personal representative), and confirmation that any restrictions on the register can be complied with. ⚠️ Raising requisitions after exchange is generally too late — a buyer who exchanges without resolving title concerns may have difficulty rescinding if they later discover a defect.
Deeds, Notices & Indefeasibility.summary_line: REQUISITION ON TITLE · BEFORE EXCHANGE · VACANT POSSESSION · SELLER'S MORTGAGE · AUTHORITY TO SELL

Notices, Restrictions & Indefeasibility.front: What is a notice on the register at HM Land Registry and how does it differ from a restriction?
Notices, Restrictions & Indefeasibility.answer: A notice is an entry in the charges register that protects an interest against a registered estate — it gives notice of the interest to a later purchaser. Types: agreed notice (both parties consent) and unilateral notice (applied for without the proprietor's consent — the proprietor can apply to cancel it). A notice protects interests such as: estate contracts, equitable easements, home rights (Family Law Act 1996). A restriction, by contrast, is entered on the proprietorship register and controls how dispositions of the registered estate can be made — it does not protect a third party interest but limits the owner's power to deal. ⚠️ A notice protects what already exists; a restriction controls what the owner can do in the future.
Notices, Restrictions & Indefeasibility.summary_line: NOTICE ON REGISTER · AGREED NOTICE · UNILATERAL NOTICE · CHARGES REGISTER · vs RESTRICTION

Notices, Restrictions & Indefeasibility.front: What is the indefeasibility principle in registered land and how does the Land Registration Act 2002 give effect to it?
Notices, Restrictions & Indefeasibility.answer: Under the LRA 2002, once a disposition is registered, the proprietor takes the estate free from all interests except: (1) overriding interests (Schs 1 and 3); (2) interests protected by a notice or restriction; (3) interests of which the proprietor has notice (in limited circumstances). This is the 'indefeasibility of title' — the register is the conclusive record of ownership and incumbrances. If a registration is made by mistake, the register may be altered or rectified (Sch 4 LRA 2002), but a proprietor in possession is protected against rectification except in cases of fraud or where it would be unjust not to rectify. Indemnity is payable if a loss results from rectification or a mistake that is not rectified (Sch 8).
Notices, Restrictions & Indefeasibility.summary_line: INDEFEASIBILITY · REGISTER · ALTERATION · SCH 4 LRA 2002 · PROPRIETOR IN POSSESSION · INDEMNITY SCH 8

Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.front: Williams & Glyn's Bank Ltd v Boland [1981]
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.issue: Whether a wife who had contributed to the purchase price of the matrimonial home, which was registered in her husband's sole name, had an overriding interest binding on the bank as mortgagee.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.rule: A person in actual occupation of registered land who has an equitable interest (e.g. under a resulting or constructive trust) has an overriding interest binding on a registered proprietor and on a purchaser or mortgagee, even if that interest is not registered.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.application: Mrs Boland had contributed financially to the purchase of the matrimonial home. The property was in Mr Boland's sole name. He mortgaged the property to the bank without her knowledge. The House of Lords held that she was in actual occupation and her equitable interest was an overriding interest binding on the bank.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.conclusion: Established the breadth of actual occupation as an overriding interest for pre-LRA 2002 law. Now codified in Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002. Banks must make enquiries of occupiers before taking a mortgage. ⚠️ The LRA 2002 narrows this slightly — an occupier who fails to disclose their interest when asked (and it was not apparent on inspection) does not have an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.summary_line: WILLIAMS v BOLAND · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · EQUITABLE INTEREST · MORTGAGEE BOUND

Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.front: Abbey National Building Society v Cann [1991]
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.issue: Whether a person could have an overriding interest based on actual occupation at the moment a mortgage was granted, where completion and mortgage occurred simultaneously.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.rule: For an overriding interest based on actual occupation to bind a mortgagee, the occupier must be in actual occupation at the relevant time — which is the moment of completion/registration of the mortgage, not the moment of exchange. Preparatory acts (e.g. moving furniture in shortly before completion) can constitute actual occupation if they are a substantial and visible act of occupation.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.application: Mrs Cann's son used her money to purchase a property, mortgaging it to Abbey National on completion. Mrs Cann began moving furniture in 35 minutes before completion. The House of Lords held that there is no 'scintilla temporis' — a moment of time — between completion and the mortgage. The mortgage and transfer are contemporaneous. She was in actual occupation, but the charge took effect simultaneously with her interest.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.conclusion: There is no scintilla temporis between a purchase and a contemporaneous mortgage — the mortgage takes effect at the same moment as the transfer. A mortgagee takes free from interests that arise only at the moment of completion. However, acts of occupation before completion can constitute actual occupation if sufficiently visible and substantial.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.summary_line: ABBEY NATIONAL v CANN · NO SCINTILLA TEMPORIS · PURCHASE AND MORTGAGE SIMULTANEOUS · ACTUAL OCCUPATION

Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.issue: Whether a wife's temporary absence from the matrimonial home (she was in hospital giving birth) meant she was not in 'actual occupation' for the purposes of an overriding interest.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.rule: Actual occupation does not require continuous physical presence. A temporary absence does not defeat a claim to actual occupation provided the person has the intention to return and has left physical evidence of their occupation.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.application: Mr Chhokar sold the matrimonial home to a third party while Mrs Chhokar was in hospital having their child. She had furniture in the property and her belongings remained there. The purchaser took with notice of her interest. The Court of Appeal held she was in actual occupation despite her absence.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.conclusion: Actual occupation is not defeated by a temporary absence if the person intends to return and their belongings/furniture remain at the property. Practical significance: a purchaser or mortgagee cannot assume that a temporarily absent occupier has no interest — physical inspection and enquiries of all occupiers remain essential.
Key Cases \u2014 Overriding Interests.summary_line: CHHOKAR v CHHOKAR · TEMPORARY ABSENCE · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · INTENTION TO RETURN · BELONGINGS REMAIN

Key Cases \u2014 Adverse Possession.issue: Whether a farmer who had been in factual possession of land for over 12 years with the intention to possess had acquired title by adverse possession under the Limitation Act 1980.
Key Cases \u2014 Adverse Possession.rule: Under the pre-LRA 2002 law, a person who factually possesses land without the owner's consent, with the intention to possess (animus possidendi), for 12 years, extinguishes the paper owner's title by adverse possession. The squatter need not have a belief in their own entitlement — an intention to possess is sufficient.
Key Cases \u2014 Adverse Possession.application: The Grahams had grazed their livestock on Pye's registered land for over 12 years under an informal arrangement. When the arrangement ended, the Grahams continued in possession. The House of Lords held the Grahams had acquired title by adverse possession — their use was factual possession with the requisite intention.
Key Cases \u2014 Adverse Possession.conclusion: Under the Limitation Act 1980 (applicable to pre-October 2003 claims on registered land and all unregistered land), 12 years' adverse possession extinguishes the paper owner's title. LRA 2002 Sch 6 changed the law for registered land from October 2003 — adverse possession now triggers a notification procedure rather than automatic title extinction. ⚠️ The case was found by the ECtHR to be compatible with the ECHR (Protocol 1 Art 1) — Pye v UK [2007].
Key Cases \u2014 Adverse Possession.summary_line: PYE v GRAHAM · ADVERSE POSSESSION · FACTUAL POSSESSION · ANIMUS POSSIDENDI · 12 YEARS · PRE-LRA 2002

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: Nina is buying a registered property. The charges register reveals a restrictive covenant from 1985 prohibiting use of the property for any purpose other than as a single private dwelling. Nina intends to run a childminding business from home. Advise her.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: The restrictive covenant binds Nina as it is protected by registration. Running a professional childminding business from the property would likely breach the covenant — it is not use as a single private dwelling. If Nina proceeds and breaches the covenant, the person(s) with the benefit of the covenant could seek an injunction to restrain the breach or claim damages. Nina should: (1) identify who has the benefit of the covenant (neighbouring landowners or a developer); (2) seek their consent; (3) consider applying to the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) under s.84 LPA 1925 to modify or discharge the covenant if it is obsolete or unreasonable; or (4) obtain restrictive covenant indemnity insurance. Nina's solicitor should not complete without advising her of this restriction and its implications.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: RESTRICTIVE COVENANT · REGISTERED · BINDING BUYER · S.84 LPA 1925 · CONSENT · INDEMNITY INSURANCE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: Tom is buying a registered property from a sole registered proprietor. The proprietorship register shows a restriction: 'No disposition of the registered estate by a sole proprietor of the registered estate (being the survivor of joint proprietors) is to be registered unless authorised by an order of the court.' What does this mean and what must Tom do?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: The restriction indicates that the property was previously held by two (or more) joint proprietors and one has died, leaving the current sole proprietor as the survivor. The restriction protects any beneficial interests that may exist in the property — for example, a co-owner who held an equitable interest under a trust. A sole trustee cannot give a valid receipt for capital money from the sale of land (LPA 1925 s.27) — a second trustee must be appointed to overreach any beneficial interests. Tom must insist on a second trustee being appointed before or on completion, so that the purchase money is paid to at least two trustees. This overreaches the beneficial interests, protecting Tom from any claim. Without a second trustee, Tom risks taking subject to an overriding interest of any beneficial owner in actual occupation.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: TRUST RESTRICTION · SOLE PROPRIETOR · TWO TRUSTEES · CAPITAL MONEY · OVERREACHING · ACTUAL OCCUPATION

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: Grace is buying a registered house. On inspecting the property she notices that the next-door neighbour appears to have a vegetable garden extending across what should be the boundary of Grace's property. How should Grace's solicitor advise her?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: This is a potential adverse possession or boundary dispute concern. Grace's solicitor should: (1) check the title plan (which is indicative, not conclusive as to exact boundaries) against the physical boundary; (2) raise a specific pre-contract enquiry with the seller about any boundary disputes or informal arrangements with the neighbour; (3) consider whether the neighbour may have acquired title by adverse possession under LRA 2002 Sch 6 (requires 10 years' adverse possession and an application to Land Registry); (4) advise Grace that the title plan cannot be relied upon for precise boundary identification — a boundary survey may be required. The seller must disclose any known boundary disputes in the TA6. Grace should not proceed without satisfactory clarification — a boundary dispute could significantly affect the use and value of the property.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: BOUNDARY DISPUTE · ADVERSE POSSESSION · TITLE PLAN INDICATIVE · TA6 ENQUIRY · BOUNDARY SURVEY

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: Liam is buying an unregistered property. A K15 land charges search reveals a Class D(ii) restrictive covenant registered against a previous owner 'John Davies'. However, the title deeds refer to the previous owner as 'John David Davies'. Is Liam bound by the covenant?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: This is a name discrepancy and it matters. Land charges in unregistered land are registered against the name of the estate owner — not against the property. A K15 search is against the name provided. If the covenant was registered against 'John David Davies' but the K15 search was run against 'John Davies', the search would not reveal it — and a purchaser who searches against the name on the deeds in good faith takes free of an entry that a correct search would have revealed. Conversely, if the covenant was correctly registered against 'John David Davies' and the search was run only against 'John Davies', the seller may have a liability for failing to disclose a registered incumbrance. Liam's solicitor should run searches against all name variants used in the title deeds to be safe and raise a requisition with the seller to clarify.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: LAND CHARGES · NAME SEARCH · NAME VARIANT · K15 · SEARCHED WRONG NAME · BOUND IF CORRECTLY REGISTERED

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: Priya is buying a registered property. During her pre-exchange inspection she meets the seller's adult son, who says he has lived in the property for three years and contributed £20,000 towards the purchase price when it was bought. The seller denies this. What should Priya's solicitor do?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: Priya's solicitor must treat this as a serious overriding interest risk. The son may have an equitable interest under a resulting or constructive trust (based on his contribution) and, being in actual occupation, may have an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002 that would bind Priya as purchaser. Priya's solicitor should: (1) make a direct enquiry of the son — ask him in writing to confirm his interest and whether he will vacate on completion; (2) advise Priya of the risk; (3) if the son asserts an interest, not proceed to exchange without the son either: (a) confirming in writing that he has no interest, or (b) executing a waiver/consent to the sale and agreeing to vacate; (4) consider whether a trust restriction should be on the register. Proceeding without resolving this exposes Priya to a claim that the son's interest is binding on her.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: ACTUAL OCCUPATION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · CONTRIBUTION · EQUITABLE INTEREST · SCH 3 PARA 2 · ENQUIRE

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: A bank takes a mortgage over a property registered in Mr Patel's sole name. Mrs Patel, who contributed to the purchase price and lives at the property, was not asked by the bank whether she had any interest. She was not separately advised. Mr Patel defaults and the bank seeks possession. Can Mrs Patel resist?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: Mrs Patel has strong grounds to resist. Under Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland [1981] and the LRA 2002 Sch 3 para 2, a person in actual occupation with an equitable interest has an overriding interest that binds a mortgagee. Mrs Patel's contribution to the purchase gives her an equitable interest under a resulting or constructive trust. Her actual occupation means this overrides the bank's charge. Furthermore, under RBS v Etridge (No 2) [2001], the bank was put on inquiry — a wife's property is being used to secure her husband's debt (effectively) — and failed to ensure she received independent legal advice. The mortgage may be voidable against Mrs Patel's share. The bank may be able to enforce against Mr Patel's beneficial share but not to obtain vacant possession against Mrs Patel without her consent or a court order.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: OVERRIDING INTEREST · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · BOLAND · ETRIDGE · INDEPENDENT ADVICE · POSSESSION RESISTED

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: An unregistered freehold property was last sold by conveyance in 1999. The seller now wants to sell to a buyer. The buyer's solicitor asks for title going back 15 years. The seller can only produce title back to 2005. Should the buyer proceed?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: The buyer should not proceed without further investigation. The seller must deduce title going back at least 15 years to a good root of title (s.44 LPA 1925). If the root is a conveyance on sale from 1999 (26 years ago), the seller should be able to produce that document. If the seller can only go back to 2005 (20 years), the buyer may be willing to accept this if the 2005 document is a good root — a conveyance on sale from 2005 is over 15 years old and would satisfy s.44. However, if the 2005 document is not a good root (e.g. it is a gift or assent), the buyer should press for the 1999 conveyance or refuse to proceed without it. Any gap in the title chain that cannot be explained raises a risk of an undisclosed incumbrance or third party interest.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.summary_line: UNREGISTERED TITLE · 15-YEAR TITLE · GOOD ROOT · CONVEYANCE ON SALE · GAP IN CHAIN · PRESS SELLER

Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.front: A local authority search on a property reveals: (1) it is in a conservation area; (2) planning permission was refused for a rear extension in 2021; (3) the road fronting the property is not adopted. Advise the buyer on each point.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Title & Searches.answer: (1) Conservation area designation means the buyer will need conservation area consent for certain works (e.g. demolition). Permitted development rights are more restricted. The buyer should factor this into their renovation plans. (2) The refused planning application is noted for information — it does not prevent a fresh application. However, it signals the local authority's attitude and the buyer should take planning advice before purchasing if they intend to extend.
