Key Cases \u2014 Termination of Leases.front: Mannai Investment Co Ltd v Eagle Star Life Assurance Co Ltd [1997]
Key Cases \u2014 Termination of Leases.issue: Whether a break notice served one day early was valid despite specifying an incorrect termination date.
Key Cases \u2014 Termination of Leases.rule: A break notice (or other contractual notice) is valid if it would be clear to a reasonable recipient, reading the notice in its context, what the tenant intended — even if the notice contains a minor error in the date. The test is objective: would a reasonable recipient understand the notice?
Key Cases \u2014 Termination of Leases.application: The lease allowed the tenant to break on 13 January 1995 by giving 6 months' notice. The tenant served notice to break on 12 January 1995 (one day early). The House of Lords (by majority) held the notice was valid — a reasonable recipient would have understood the tenant intended to exercise the break on 13 January.
Key Cases \u2014 Termination of Leases.conclusion: Minor errors in break notices do not automatically invalidate them — the court applies an objective test of what a reasonable recipient would understand. However, courts have also strictly construed break conditions (compliance with rent payment, vacant possession) — the Mannai principle addresses the form of the notice, not the conditions for exercise. ⚠️ Advise clients to exercise break clauses precisely — both in wording and in satisfying all conditions.
Key Cases \u2014 Termination of Leases.summary_line: MANNAI v EAGLE STAR · BREAK NOTICE · MINOR ERROR · REASONABLE RECIPIENT TEST · OBJECTIVE · VALID

Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.front: Expert Clothing Service & Sales Ltd v Hillgate House Ltd [1986]
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.issue: Whether a breach of a covenant to reconstruct and fit out premises by a specified date was remediable for the purposes of s.146 LPA 1925.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.rule: A breach of a positive covenant (one requiring the tenant to do something) is generally remediable — the breach can be remedied by carrying out the required act. A landlord must give a s.146 notice and allow a reasonable time to remedy before forfeiting. A breach of a negative covenant (not to do something) may be irremediable if the harm cannot be undone.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.application: The tenant covenanted to reconstruct and fit out the premises by a certain date and failed to do so. The Court of Appeal held this was a remediable breach — the tenant could still carry out the reconstruction (albeit late). The landlord could not forfeit without first serving a s.146 notice and allowing time to remedy.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.conclusion: The remediable/irremediable distinction is fact-specific. Positive covenant breaches (failure to do something) are usually remediable. Negative covenant breaches (doing a prohibited act) may be irremediable if the damage is permanent. ⚠️ Even an irremediable breach requires a s.146 notice — it just need not allow time to remedy.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.summary_line: EXPERT CLOTHING · S.146 LPA 1925 · REMEDIABLE BREACH · POSITIVE COVENANT · TIME TO REMEDY

Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.issue: Whether a tenant could still apply for relief from forfeiture after the landlord had effected peaceable re-entry.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.rule: A tenant may apply for relief from forfeiture under s.146(2) LPA 1925 after peaceable re-entry, not only before or during court proceedings. The court retains jurisdiction to grant relief until a new lease has been granted or the position has become irreversible.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.application: The landlord forfeited by peaceable re-entry (changing the locks) after the tenant breached a covenant to carry out works. The tenant then applied for relief. The House of Lords held that relief was available even after peaceable re-entry — s.146(2) was not limited to cases where court proceedings had been commenced.
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.conclusion: Tenants can apply for relief from forfeiture after peaceable re-entry. Practically, this makes peaceable re-entry riskier for commercial landlords — the tenant can apply for relief and potentially be reinstated. The court has a wide discretion and will consider the conduct of both parties. ⚠️ Peaceable re-entry is generally only permissible for commercial premises — for residential premises, the landlord must obtain a court order (Protection from Eviction Act 1977).
Key Cases \u2014 Forfeiture.summary_line: BILLSON v RESIDENTIAL APARTMENTS · RELIEF FROM FORFEITURE · AFTER PEACEABLE RE-ENTRY · S.146(2)

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A residential leaseholder has 72 years remaining on their lease. They wish to sell. The buyer's mortgage lender requires at least 70 years unexpired after completion. The buyer's solicitor raises the issue. What should the leaseholder's solicitor advise?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: The leaseholder should extend the lease before or simultaneously with the sale. With 72 years remaining, the lease just meets the lender's minimum requirement, but is dangerously close to the threshold. A statutory lease extension under the LRHUDA 1993 adds 90 years to the unexpired term (giving 162 years) and reduces the ground rent to a peppercorn. The cost (premium) is calculated by statutory formula — at 72 years the premium will be relatively modest because the term has not yet fallen below 80 years (where 'marriage value' enters the calculation). Options: (1) extend the lease before exchange — the seller can serve a s.42 notice and complete the extension before the sale proceeds; (2) assign the s.42 notice to the buyer so the buyer can continue the extension after completion. The solicitor should advise the client to extend sooner rather than later — below 80 years the premium increases significantly.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: LEASE EXTENSION · BELOW 80 YEARS · MARRIAGE VALUE · LRHUDA 1993 · +90 YEARS · PEPPERCORN · PREMIUM

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A commercial landlord discovers that the tenant is using the premises for an unauthorised purpose in breach of the user covenant. The landlord immediately sends a letter demanding the tenant vacate and changes the locks the next morning. The tenant had been paying rent, which the landlord accepted last week. Advise both parties.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: The landlord has acted unlawfully in two respects. First, waiver: by accepting rent last week with knowledge of the breach, the landlord waived the right to forfeit for this particular breach. Second, even if waiver had not occurred, peaceable re-entry without first serving a s.146 LPA 1925 notice is unlawful — a s.146 notice must be served for breach of a covenant other than non-payment of rent, specifying the breach and (if remediable) requiring remedy within a reasonable time. The tenant is entitled to: (1) apply for relief from forfeiture — the court will likely grant this given the waiver and procedural defects; (2) claim damages for unlawful eviction. The landlord must allow the tenant back in and restart the process correctly — serve a s.146 notice, refrain from accepting rent, and wait to see if the breach is remedied before proceeding.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: WAIVER · UNLAWFUL EVICTION · S.146 NOT SERVED · RELIEF FROM FORFEITURE · PEACEABLE RE-ENTRY

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A residential flat owner receives a service charge demand for £15,000 for major external works. The freeholder did not carry out any s.20 consultation before undertaking the works. The leaseholder refuses to pay more than £250. Is the freeholder entitled to the full £15,000?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: No. Under s.20 LTA 1985, where the costs of qualifying works exceed £250 per leaseholder, the landlord must carry out a formal consultation process before undertaking the works. Failure to consult means the landlord can only recover £250 from each leaseholder regardless of the actual cost (unless a dispensation from the First-tier Tribunal is obtained). The leaseholder's refusal to pay more than £250 is legally correct. The landlord's remedy is to apply to the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) for a dispensation from the consultation requirements — the Tribunal has discretion to grant this if it is satisfied that it is just and equitable to do so, having regard to any prejudice caused to the leaseholder. Without a dispensation, the landlord cannot recover more than £250.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: S.20 CONSULTATION · £250 CAP · FAILURE TO CONSULT · FTT DISPENSATION · SERVICE CHARGES LTA 1985

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A tenant assigns a post-1995 lease to an assignee. As a condition of consent, the landlord requires the outgoing tenant to enter an AGA guaranteeing the assignee's obligations. Two years later, the assignee assigns onward to a third party. Is the original tenant still bound by the AGA?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: No — the original tenant (the AGA guarantor) is released when the immediate assignee (the person they guaranteed) assigns onward. Under the LT(C)A 1995, an AGA only guarantees the obligations of the immediately following assignee. When the assignee makes a further assignment, the AGA automatically terminates. The original tenant has no ongoing liability to the landlord in respect of the second assignee or any future assignees. The landlord may require the first assignee to provide their own AGA when they assign onward (guaranteeing the second assignee) — but that is a separate matter between the landlord and the first assignee. ⚠️ This is the key distinction from pre-1996 leases, where the original tenant could remain liable throughout the entire term regardless of assignments.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: AGA · IMMEDIATE ASSIGNEE · RELEASE ON FURTHER ASSIGNMENT · LT(C)A 1995 · ONE ASSIGNMENT ONLY

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A residential tenant holds a weekly periodic tenancy jointly with their partner. The relationship breaks down. Without the partner's knowledge, the tenant serves a notice to quit on the landlord. The partner wants to stay. Can the landlord rely on the notice?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: Yes — the landlord can rely on the notice. Under Hammersmith & Fulham LBC v Monk [1992], one joint periodic tenant can unilaterally serve a valid notice to quit that terminates the entire joint tenancy. The other joint tenant cannot prevent this. A periodic tenancy requires a fresh act of will at the start of each period — if one tenant withdraws their will (by serving notice), the tenancy cannot continue as a joint tenancy. The partner who wishes to stay has no right to insist the tenancy continues — they must seek alternative housing. ⚠️ The landlord should ensure the notice complies formally (correct length and addressing) before relying on it. This rule can operate harshly — it has been challenged under Art 8 ECHR but upheld in subsequent cases.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: JOINT PERIODIC TENANCY · UNILATERAL NOTICE TO QUIT · HAMMERSMITH v MONK · VALID · ART 8 ECHR

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A commercial landlord has opted to tax a property. The tenant's 10-year lease expires and the tenant exercises their LTA 1954 right to a new tenancy. The new tenancy is ordered by the court on the same terms as the old lease. The tenant argues the rent under the new lease should not include VAT as the new lease was 'on the same terms'. Is the tenant correct?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: No — the tenant is not correct. The court ordering a new tenancy 'on the same terms' relates to the covenants and conditions in the lease, not to the VAT position. VAT is a statutory imposition determined by the landlord's VAT election and the relevant legislation — it is not a lease term. If the landlord has a valid option to tax in place, they are entitled to charge VAT on the rent under the new tenancy regardless of whether the old lease provided for VAT. The option to tax survives the grant of a new lease. The tenant should have checked the VAT position during negotiations for the new tenancy and sought either removal of the option to tax (which requires HMRC consent after 20 years) or a price adjustment. The new lease will attract VAT at the same rate as the old one.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: LTA 1954 · SAME TERMS · NEW TENANCY · VAT IS STATUTORY NOT LEASE TERM · OPTION TO TAX SURVIVES

Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.front: A commercial tenant has a lease that is contracted out of the LTA 1954. The lease expires. The tenant refuses to leave and continues paying rent, which the landlord accepts for three months. Has the landlord accidentally granted a new tenancy with security of tenure?
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.answer: No — security of tenure has not been restored. A new periodic tenancy arising from holding over after expiry of a contracted-out lease is itself a new tenancy. However, for the new tenancy to have security of tenure under the LTA 1954, the contracting-out procedure would need to have been carried out again before the new tenancy was granted. Since the landlord accepted rent without going through that procedure, a new implied periodic tenancy has arisen — but is it contracted out? Courts have held that a tenant holding over after a contracted-out lease takes an implied periodic tenancy that is also outside the 1954 Act if it can be shown the parties' intention was to continue the contracted-out arrangement. However, this is uncertain — the safest approach is for the landlord to either serve a notice to quit promptly, refuse rent, or grant a formal contracted-out lease immediately. Accepting rent without a clear position is risky.
Scenario Cards \u2014 Leasehold.summary_line: CONTRACTED OUT · HOLDING OVER · IMPLIED PERIODIC TENANCY · ACCEPT RENT RISK · SERVE NOTICE TO QUIT

AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.front: What are a solicitor's obligations when they suspect their conveyancing client is involved in mortgage fraud?
AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.answer: If a solicitor suspects mortgage fraud: (1) they must not assist in the fraud — participating would make them complicit in a criminal offence; (2) they must cease to act where there is a conflict between the buyer client and the lender client; (3) they cannot disclose the reason for ceasing to act (confidentiality), but must notify the lender they are no longer acting; (4) they must consider whether a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) is required under POCA 2002 — if they know or suspect that property represents the proceeds of crime, they must file a SAR and obtain DAML consent before completing; (5) tipping off the client that a SAR has been filed is a criminal offence. ⚠️ Continuing to act and certifying a false mortgage application would expose the solicitor to criminal liability and SRA disciplinary proceedings.
AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.summary_line: MORTGAGE FRAUD · SOLICITOR OBLIGATIONS · CEASE TO ACT · SAR · POCA 2002 · NO FALSE CERTIFICATION

AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.front: What is a NHBC Buildmark warranty and what protection does it provide to a new build buyer?
AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.answer: The NHBC Buildmark warranty is a 10-year structural warranty provided by the National House Building Council (NHBC) for newly constructed residential properties. It provides: (1) Years 1–2 (Builder's Period) — the builder is responsible for remedying defects caused by failure to build to NHBC standards; if the builder fails, NHBC steps in; (2) Years 3–10 (Insurance Period) — NHBC insures against major structural damage; (3) Deposit protection — if the builder becomes insolvent before completion, the deposit is protected. The warranty transfers to subsequent buyers during the 10-year period. ⚠️ The Buildmark warranty does not cover wear and tear, general maintenance, or defects caused by the owner — it covers structural defects arising from poor workmanship or materials.
AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.summary_line: NHBC BUILDMARK · 10 YEAR WARRANTY · BUILDER PERIOD 2 YEARS · INSURANCE PERIOD 3-10 YEARS · DEPOSIT

AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.front: What is contaminated land and what liability can it create for a buyer of commercial property?
AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.answer: Contaminated land is defined under s.78A Environmental Protection Act 1990 as land where substances present in, on, or under the land cause significant harm or significant possibility of such harm, or where there is pollution of controlled waters. Liability: (1) the local authority may serve a remediation notice on 'appropriate persons' — usually the polluter ('polluter pays') or, if the polluter cannot be found, the current owner/occupier; (2) a buyer who knowingly acquires contaminated land with awareness of the contamination may be the 'appropriate person' liable to remediate; (3) the remediation costs can be substantial. ⚠️ An environmental search and potentially a Phase 1 (and Phase 2) environmental survey are essential in commercial transactions — particularly for industrial sites or brownfield land.
AML, Conflicts & Client Obligations.summary_line: CONTAMINATED LAND · S.78A EPA 1990 · REMEDIATION NOTICE · POLLUTER PAYS · APPROPRIATE PERSON · DUE DILIGENCE

Key Cases \u2014 Chancel Repair.front: Aston Cantlow and Wilmcote with Billesley Parochial Church Council v Wallbank [2003]
Key Cases \u2014 Chancel Repair.issue: Whether chancel repair liability — an ancient obligation on owners of rectorial land to repair the chancel of a parish church — was compatible with the Human Rights Act 1998 and enforceable against private landowners.
Key Cases \u2014 Chancel Repair.rule: Chancel repair liability is a proprietary burden on the land, not a personal obligation. It runs with the land and binds successors in title. A Parochial Church Council (PCC) is not a core public authority and is not directly bound by the HRA 1998 in exercising its right to enforce chancel repair liability against a private landowner.
Key Cases \u2014 Chancel Repair.application: The Wallbanks purchased a farm without investigating chancel repair liability. The PCC demanded contribution to the cost of repairs to the chancel. The House of Lords held the liability was enforceable — the PCC was not a public authority for HRA purposes when enforcing a private law property right.
Key Cases \u2014 Chancel Repair.conclusion: Chancel repair liability is enforceable against private landowners on affected rectorial land. Since October 2013, it must be registered at Land Registry to bind purchasers of registered land. Unregistered chancel liability does not bind a purchaser of registered land after that date. ⚠️ Chancel searches are still advisable in rural areas — if a notice is registered, the buyer takes subject to it. Indemnity insurance is available where the liability is unregistered but the risk cannot be entirely discounted.
Key Cases \u2014 Chancel Repair.summary_line: ASTON CANTLOW v WALLBANK · CHANCEL REPAIR · PROPRIETARY BURDEN · OCTOBER 2013 REGISTRATION · HRA

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is an equitable lease under Walsh v Lonsdale truly equivalent to a legal lease?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
The orthodox position following Walsh v Lonsdale (1882) is that equity treats a specifically enforceable contract for a lease as equivalent to the lease itself. Yet this equivalence is imperfect in several respects.

First, an equitable lease is a merely personal right until the legal lease is granted — it does not bind a bona fide purchaser of the legal estate for value without notice (in unregistered land) or a disponee who takes a registered title free of unprotected equitable interests (in registered land). A legal lease, being a legal interest, binds the world.

Second, the availability of equitable remedies is discretionary. Specific performance — the mechanism that underpins the Walsh v Lonsdale doctrine — will not be granted where the claimant has acted unconscionably, where damages are adequate, or where the contract is specifically unenforceable for some reason. The 'legal lease equivalent' therefore rests on a foundation that can be withdrawn.

Third, a legal periodic tenancy may arise from conduct (e.g. payment and acceptance of rent) even where the parties intended an equitable lease. The interaction between the equitable lease and the legal periodic tenancy can produce uncertainty about which instrument governs the parties' relationship.

Professor Bridge ('The Primacy of the Register', Conv 2012) has argued that the LRA 2002 significantly reduces the Walsh v Lonsdale equivalence for registered land — the register is the source of title and unregistered equitable leases sit uneasily within a system that purports to make the register conclusive. The unresolved tension: should a contractual tenant in occupation rely on their actual occupation as an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002, or does the Bruton v London & Quadrant Housing Trust line suggest that the contractual relationship is sufficient? The two doctrines pull in different directions on the nature of leasehold interests.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: WALSH V LONSDALE · EQUITABLE LEASE · SPECIFICALLY ENFORCEABLE · LEGAL LEASE EQUIVALENT · LIMITS — THIRD PARTIES · BONA FIDE PURCHASER · TULK V MOXHAY

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the adverse possession regime under LRA 2002 Schedule 6 too protective of registered owners?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
The LRA 2002 fundamentally changed adverse possession for registered land. Under the old law (Limitation Act 1980), 12 years' adverse possession extinguished the registered proprietor's title automatically. The new regime under Sch 6 requires the squatter to apply to Land Registry after 10 years, which triggers a notification to the registered proprietor who then has two years to object and bring possession proceedings. Only if the proprietor fails to act within that period does the squatter obtain registration.

Proponents argue the new system is fairer — it removes the 'windfall' for squatters and protects property rights more robustly, consistent with Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR (peaceful enjoyment of possessions). The old automatic extinction was held compatible with the ECHR in JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v UK [2007] GC (reversing the Chamber's finding of a violation), but by a narrow majority.

Critics, including Professor Martin Dixon ('Adverse Possession and the Land Registration Act 2002: A Hatchet Job', [2003] 67 Conv 302), argue the new regime overprotects registered owners who may have entirely forgotten about land, abandoned it, or have no legitimate expectation of reclaiming it. The two-year response window favours well-resourced institutional landowners who monitor their portfolios, not individual absentee owners. Furthermore, the 'estoppel' exception in Sch 6 para 5(c) — where it would be unconscionable to dispossess the squatter — is narrowly construed, leaving many long-term occupiers without adequate protection.

The unresolved tension: should adverse possession be primarily about penalising dormant ownership (the old approach) or about protecting the integrity of the register (the new approach)? These produce fundamentally different outcomes and the LRA 2002 has not ended academic debate on the proper function of limitation in land law.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: ADVERSE POSSESSION · LRA 2002 SCHEDULE 6 · REGISTERED PROPRIETOR NOTIFIED · 10-YEAR PERIOD · LIMITATION ACT 1980 REPLACED · BEAULANE · LAW COMMISSION

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Do overriding interests undermine the reliability of the register under the LRA 2002?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
Overriding interests represent the most significant tension in registered land law: the LRA 2002's aim of making the register a 'mirror' of title is directly compromised by interests that bind purchasers without appearing on the register.

The Law Commission's report 'Land Registration for the Twenty-First Century' (Law Com No 271, 2001) — the report that led to the LRA 2002 — acknowledged this tension. It sought to reduce the categories of overriding interest (e.g. by phasing out legal easements and profits acquired before 2003 as overriding interests) and to narrow actual occupation under Sch 3 para 2 by adding the 'apparent on inspection' qualification. However, critics including Professor Cooke ('E-Conveyancing in England and Wales', 2004) argue these reforms were insufficient.

The actual occupation category under Sch 3 para 2 remains the most litigated and contested overriding interest. Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland [1981] demonstrated that it can defeat sophisticated institutional lenders. The 'apparent on inspection' qualification adds some protection for purchasers, but 'apparent' is not defined in the Act and has been construed broadly — a person's belongings in a property may suffice (Chhokar v Chhokar [1984]).

Academics including Professor Gray ('Elements of Land Law', 5th ed) have argued that a fully reliable register would require abolition of all overriding interests — forcing all third party interests to be registered. Against this, Professor Dixon has noted that this would place intolerable burdens on informal rights holders who may lack legal advice or sophistication. The unresolved question: can a registration system that aspires to completeness coexist with a doctrine that protects the unregistered rights of vulnerable occupiers? The LRA 2002 chose compromise — and the debate continues.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: OVERRIDING INTERESTS · REGISTER RELIABILITY · ACTUAL OCCUPATION · WILLIAMS V GLYNS BANK · LRA 2002 S.29 · CURTAILMENT · THORNTON · LAW COMMISSION

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Can a lease exist without a proprietary estate? The academic debate on the Bruton tenancy.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
Bruton v London & Quadrant Housing Trust [2000] HL held that a tenancy (applying the Street v Mountford criteria) can arise as a purely contractual arrangement even where the grantor has no estate in the land. Lord Hoffmann characterised the tenancy as a contractual relationship binding only the parties, not as a proprietary interest.

This analysis has been sharply criticised. Professor Bright ('Leases, Exclusive Possession and Estates', (2000) 116 LQR 7) argues that the orthodox understanding of a lease is as a proprietary interest — a term of years absolute — and that the House of Lords' reasoning creates a 'non-estate lease' that is conceptually incoherent. If a Bruton tenancy is not a proprietary interest, it cannot be an overriding interest, cannot be registered, and does not run with the land — yet tenancy law (repairing obligations, quiet enjoyment, security of tenure) has always been premised on the existence of a proprietary relationship.

Professor Pawlowski and Professor Brown ('Bruton Tenancies — A Unique Form of Tenure', [2013] Conv 421) have argued that Bruton tenancies fill an important social gap — they enable social landlords and charities to grant occupiers legally enforceable rights even without a formal estate. Without Bruton, occupiers in precarious housing would have mere licences and no repairing rights.

The Law Commission in 'Renting Homes' (Law Com No 284, 2003) took no definitive position on Bruton but acknowledged the uncertainty it creates for registered land. The unresolved tension: is a lease an estate or a contract? If the former, Bruton is wrong. If leases can be both, the boundary between licence and lease becomes still more uncertain.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: BRUTON TENANCY · NO PROPRIETARY ESTATE · CONTRACTUAL TENANCY · STREET V MOUNTFORD · LONDON AND QUADRANT · GRAY AND GRAY · PROPRIETARY PARADOX

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Should the leasehold system in England and Wales be abolished?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
Leasehold has been a feature of English land law for centuries, but has attracted sustained political and academic criticism, particularly for residential long leases.

The case against leasehold is articulated by the Law Commission in 'Leasehold Home Ownership: Buying Your Freehold or Extending Your Lease' (Law Com No 392, 2020): ground rent escalation clauses (now prohibited for new leases by the Leasehold Reform (Ground Rent) Act 2022), service charge opacity, management failings, and the costs and complexity of enfranchisement all harm leaseholders. Proponents of abolition (including the Law Society's 2018 consultation response) argue that commonhold — introduced by the Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Act 2002 — provides a superior model: each flat owner holds the freehold of their unit as part of a commonhold community, with shared obligations governed by a community statement. There is no 'landlord'.

Proponents of retention argue that leasehold provides necessary mechanisms for managing multi-occupancy buildings — service charges, building insurance, and enforcement of obligations — that commonhold has not yet demonstrated it can replicate efficiently. They point to the very low adoption of commonhold since 2002 (fewer than 20 commonhold developments registered) as evidence that the market does not favour it.

The Law Commission's 2020 report recommends making enfranchisement cheaper and easier rather than abolishing leasehold outright, and recommends reforms to make commonhold more workable for existing leasehold conversions. The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024 extended enfranchisement rights. The unresolved tension: whether legislative reform of leasehold can adequately address its structural problems, or whether only abolition and replacement with commonhold will provide long-term consumer protection.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: LEASEHOLD ABOLITION · LEASEHOLD REFORM · ENFRANCHISEMENT · COMMONHOLD · LEASEHOLD REFORM HOUSING AND URBAN DEVELOPMENT ACT 1993 · LAW COMMISSION 2020

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Has the law struck the right balance between protecting sureties and facilitating secured lending post-Etridge?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001] HL codified the obligations of banks to protect themselves against transactions procured by undue influence. Lord Nicholls' judgment requires banks to insist on independent legal advice, communicate directly with the solicitor, and receive written confirmation. Critics and proponents have evaluated this framework from different angles.

Professor Fehlberg ('Sexually Transmitted Debt', 1997) argued before Etridge that the law was inadequate to protect women (disproportionately affected by surety transactions), because requiring only that advice be given ignored the practical reality of economic dependency and social pressure that makes independent advice ineffective. Post-Etridge, Chen-Wishart ('Undue Influence: Vindicating Relationships of Influence', [2006] CLP) argues that the Etridge protocol has become a compliance checklist rather than a genuine protective mechanism — solicitors certify advice was given without probing whether it was truly understood.

From the banking perspective, Hooley ('Undue Influence' in 'Chitty on Contracts') argues that the Etridge requirements impose costs and complexity on consumer lending, making secured credit more expensive and less accessible, without eliminating undue influence — it merely shifts liability to solicitors. The solicitor who fails to give adequate advice becomes the defendant rather than the bank.

The unresolved tension: the Etridge protocol is procedural rather than substantive. It cannot protect a surety who understands but freely chooses to proceed under pressure. Academic consensus (see Bigwood, 'Undue Influence in the House of Lords', (2002) 65 MLR 435) is that English law has not resolved whether undue influence is primarily about protecting autonomy or about policing unconscionable conduct — and this ambiguity continues to generate litigation.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: UNDUE INFLUENCE · ETRIDGE NO 2 · BANK'S OBLIGATIONS · CERTIFICATE OF INDEPENDENT ADVICE · O'BRIEN · SURETY PROTECTION · UNCONSCIONABILITY

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the law of restrictive covenants in need of fundamental reform?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
The law of restrictive covenants running with the land in equity — the doctrine in Tulk v Moxhay (1848) — has been criticised as insufficiently coherent and practically burdensome since at least the Law Commission's 1967 report.

The Law Commission's most recent consultation, 'Making Land Work: Easements, Covenants and Profits à Prendre' (Law Com No 327, 2011), recommended replacing the current equitable regime with statutory 'land obligations' — a new category of legal interest that would bind successors as of right without the need for complex equitable rules about the running of benefit and burden. The report also recommended a simpler mechanism for discharging obsolete covenants before the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) under s.84 LPA 1925, which critics argue is too expensive and slow.

Professor Megarry and Wade ('The Law of Real Property', 9th ed.) have long noted the paradox that negative covenants run in equity (Tulk v Moxhay) while positive covenants cannot run at law or in equity (Halsall v Brizell [1957] being an exception based on benefit and burden). This creates asymmetry: a developer can impose a covenant against building but cannot impose an obligation to maintain a shared driveway in a way that binds successors. Practitioners use workarounds (indemnity chains, rentcharges, estate management schemes) that are expensive and fragile.

The 2011 Report's recommendations have not been implemented. The unresolved tension: the common law's resistance to positive obligations running with land (unlike civil law systems, where obligations personnelles réelles are well-established) means that English land law lacks a simple mechanism for neighbourhood-level governance of obligations — a gap that generates both litigation and transactional complexity.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS · TULK V MOXHAY · LAW COMMISSION · LAND OBLIGATIONS · POSITIVE COVENANTS DO NOT RUN · REFORM PROPOSALS · HALSALL V BRIZELL

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the LTA 1954 security of tenure regime still fit for purpose in the modern commercial property market?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
The Landlord and Tenant Act 1954 Pt II was enacted in post-war conditions where tenants needed protection from arbitrary eviction. Its continued application to commercial tenancies has attracted sustained debate.

Arguments for retention: Professor Reynolds ('Leasehold Disputes', [2009] Conv 449) argues that the 1954 Act gives small businesses — the archetypal commercial tenant — essential protection against market power imbalances with institutional landlords. Without renewal rights, short-term leases would expose small tenants to arbitrary rent uplift or eviction at renewal, deterring investment in business premises and leasehold improvements.

Arguments for reform or abolition: The British Property Federation and Investment Property Forum have long argued that the 1954 Act distorts the commercial property market — it creates uncertainty for landlords who wish to redevelop, reduces flexibility in lease structuring, and imposes litigation costs when grounds for opposition are contested. The ability to contract out (post-2004 RRO) has reduced the Act's impact, but contracting out requires procedural compliance and the Act still governs a substantial proportion of commercial leases.

Comparatively, most civil law systems (France, Germany, the Netherlands) provide commercial tenants with renewal rights broadly equivalent to the 1954 Act — England is not out of step internationally. However, English market practice has moved heavily towards shorter leases (5 years) compared to the traditional 25-year lease, which the 1954 Act was designed to regulate.

The Law Commission has not recently reviewed the 1954 Act. The unresolved tension is whether security of tenure for commercial tenants is a legitimate market intervention (given the illiquidity and long-term nature of commercial property) or a distortion that inflates the costs of commercial occupation and reduces flexibility for both parties.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: LTA 1954 · SECURITY OF TENURE · COMMERCIAL TENANTS · LAW COMMISSION 1992 · CONTRACTING OUT · RENEWAL RIGHTS · MODERN FITNESS · LCCP 208

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is chancel repair liability an anachronistic burden incompatible with modern property law?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
Chancel repair liability is one of the oldest surviving proprietary burdens in English law — it predates the Reformation and arises from the historic obligation of rectorial landowners to maintain the chancel of a parish church. Its survival into the 21st century has attracted both legal and constitutional criticism.

The Human Rights Act 1998 dimension was tested in Aston Cantlow PCC v Wallbank [2003] HL. The House of Lords held that the PCC was not a core public authority and therefore not directly bound by the HRA 1998 when enforcing a private law obligation. This reasoning was criticised by commentators including Professor Grosz and Professor Beatson as drawing an excessively narrow definition of 'public authority' — the Church of England exercises a quasi-public function and chancel repair liability operates like a tax.

The European Court of Human Rights in Aston Cantlow PCC v UK [2004] Admissibility declined to find a violation of Art 1 Protocol 1 (peaceful enjoyment of property) on the facts, but the case prompted Parliament to act: after October 2013, unregistered chancel repair liability no longer binds purchasers of registered land. Existing liabilities must be protected by a notice on the register.

Professor Hopkins ('Chancel Repairs', [2003] Conv 491) has argued that even registered chancel liabilities are indefensible — they are ancient remnants that impose costs arbitrarily on specific landowners with no rational connection to the maintenance of religious buildings in which they have no interest. The argument from property rights is that burdens should be imposed by legislation, not inherited from pre-Reformation ecclesiastical law. The unresolved tension: chancel liabilities that were registered before October 2013 remain binding in perpetuity, leaving pockets of liability that will generate disputes for decades.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: CHANCEL REPAIR LIABILITY · ASTON CANTLOW · HUMAN RIGHTS · WALLBANK · ARTICLE 1 PROTOCOL 1 · OVERRIDING INTEREST PRE-2013 · REGISTRATION NOW REQUIRED

Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Does the VAT treatment of commercial property — particularly the option to tax and TOGC — create unnecessary complexity and unfairness?
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.answer:
The VAT treatment of commercial property is widely regarded as one of the most complex areas of UK tax law, and has been the subject of sustained criticism from practitioners, the Law Society, and the Bar.

The core criticism is the 'exempt by default, opt to tax' structure. Unlike residential property (zero-rated on new builds, exempt on resales), commercial property starts as exempt — meaning the landlord/seller cannot recover input VAT on construction or refurbishment costs. To achieve tax neutrality, they must opt to tax, making their supplies standard-rated. This creates a mismatch problem: an opted-to-tax property becomes significantly more expensive for an unregistered buyer or a VAT-exempt occupier (e.g. a bank, insurance company, or charity).

Professor Gammie ('VAT and Property', Butterworths, 2019) argues that the current regime does not follow a coherent principle — the distinction between new and existing buildings, the 20-year irrevocability of options, and the complexity of TOGC conditions reflect historical accident rather than principled design. The TOGC regime itself is poorly understood by practitioners: the simultaneous opt-to-tax requirement before completion is a trap that regularly causes transactions to fail or VAT to be accidentally chargeable.

The Institute for Fiscal Studies in its 2011 Mirrlees Review recommended simplification of the VAT treatment of property — in particular, extending zero-rating to all new construction and abolishing the exempt/opt-to-tax distinction. This would align UK practice more closely with other EU VAT systems. The unresolved tension: simplification would remove planning opportunities (the option to tax allows some sellers to recover input VAT they would otherwise lose), and any transition from the current regime would create substantial winners and losers in the existing property market.
Property Practice \u2014 Academic Depth.summary_line: VAT ON COMMERCIAL PROPERTY · OPTION TO TAX · TOGC · COMPLEXITY · EXEMPT SUPPLY · STANDARD RATED · INPUT TAX RECOVERY · OTS REVIEW

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Alex exchanges contracts to buy a house on Monday. On Thursday — before completion — a fire breaks out and causes £40,000 of damage to the property. Alex has not yet arranged buildings insurance because he thought there was no need to insure until he got the keys and legal title passed to him on completion.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Risk passes to the buyer at completion — or at least when legal title passes. Alex has no legal interest yet, so the loss must fall on the seller who still owns the property.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Risk passes to the buyer at exchange of contracts, not at completion. Under the Standard Conditions of Sale (5th ed.) condition 5.1, the buyer bears the risk of damage to the property from the moment of exchange. Legal title is irrelevant to the allocation of risk — the seller holds the property on constructive trust for the buyer from exchange.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the common conflation of 'risk' with 'legal ownership'. The fact that Alex does not yet have legal title (which passes at completion) does not defer the transfer of risk. The SCS allocates risk to the buyer at exchange as a contractual matter. Alex's assumption that he need not insure until completion is wrong — the standard advice is to insure from exchange.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Risk passes to the buyer at exchange under the Standard Conditions of Sale — insure from exchange, not completion. Legal title and risk pass at different moments.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: RISK ON EXCHANGE · STANDARD CONDITIONS OF SALE · NOT COMPLETION · PROP TRAP — RISK PASSES AT EXCHANGE · SC 7.2.1 · INSURANCE · BUYER AT RISK

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Contracts provide for completion on 15 March. The buyer fails to transfer funds and completion does not take place. The seller immediately treats the contract as terminated, re-lists the property, and accepts a new offer. The seller's solicitor tells the original buyer that the contract is at an end.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Time is of the essence for the completion date in a conveyancing contract — failure to complete on the due date entitles the innocent party to rescind immediately.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Time is not automatically of the essence for the completion date in a conveyancing contract. Under the Standard Conditions of Sale, the innocent party must first serve a Notice to Complete, which makes time of the essence and gives the defaulting party 10 working days to complete. Only after expiry of that notice without completion can the innocent party rescind.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the intuitive assumption that a specified completion date is a firm deadline — in commercial contracts generally, time may be of the essence, but in residential conveyancing under the SCS it is not. By purporting to rescind without serving a Notice to Complete, the seller has itself committed a repudiatory breach — the buyer could accept that breach and sue the seller.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Under the Standard Conditions of Sale, time is not of the essence for the completion date. To rescind for non-completion, the innocent party must first serve a valid Notice to Complete (giving 10 working days). Rescinding without notice is itself a breach.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: TIME OF THE ESSENCE · STANDARD CONDITIONS · NOT AUTOMATIC · NOTICE TO COMPLETE · SC 6.8 · 10 WORKING DAYS · PROP TRAP — TIME NOT AUTOMATICALLY ESSENTIAL

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Deborah buys a house for £320,000. As part of the deal, she pays the seller £200,000 in cash and takes over the seller's outstanding mortgage of £120,000. Her solicitor calculates SDLT as: 0% on £200,000 = nil. Deborah is delighted to learn she owes no SDLT.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: SDLT is calculated only on the cash element of the consideration — assuming the mortgage is the seller's problem and not part of the purchase price for tax purposes.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: SDLT is charged on the total chargeable consideration, which includes both cash paid and the value of any debt assumed by the buyer. Deborah's chargeable consideration is £320,000 (£200,000 cash + £120,000 mortgage assumed). SDLT: 0% on £250,000 = £0; 5% on £70,000 = £3,500. Total SDLT: £3,500 — not nil.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the intuition that the buyer's 'cost' is the cash they hand over. But SDLT is a tax on the economic transaction, not just the cash payment. Assuming a debt is economic value received by the seller — it extinguishes their liability — and therefore forms part of the consideration the buyer gives. Calculating on the cash element alone is a common and costly error.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: SDLT chargeable consideration includes any debt assumed by the buyer, not just cash paid. Always aggregate cash + debt assumed to find the total chargeable consideration before applying the SDLT rates.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: SDLT · CHARGEABLE CONSIDERATION · DEBT ASSUMED · S.43 FA 2003 · PROP TRAP — DEBT COUNTS AS CONSIDERATION · NOT CASH ONLY · TOTAL CONSIDERATION

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Fiona buys a residential property for £950,000. Her solicitor calculates SDLT as: 10% × £950,000 = £95,000. Fiona is surprised by the large bill and asks whether this is correct.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: SDLT is calculated by applying the highest applicable rate to the entire purchase price — like income tax brackets work in some jurisdictions.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: SDLT is calculated on a slice basis — each rate applies only to the portion of the purchase price falling within that band. For £950,000: 0% on £0–£250,000 = £0; 5% on £250,001–£925,000 (£675,000) = £33,750; 10% on £925,001–£950,000 (£25,000) = £2,500. Total SDLT = £36,250 — not £95,000.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits confusion between a flat rate (apply one rate to the whole) and a slice system (each rate applies to only that portion of value). SDLT uses a slice system identical in structure to income tax rates. The 10% rate applies only to the slice between £925,001 and £1,500,000 — not to the entire £950,000 price.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: SDLT is calculated on a slice basis — each rate applies only to the portion of the price within that band. Never apply the highest applicable rate to the whole purchase price.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: SDLT · SLICE BASIS · NOT SLAB · PROP TRAP — EACH RATE APPLIES TO BAND ONLY · RESIDENTIAL RATES · GRADUATED · FINANCE ACT 2003

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

George owns a buy-to-let flat. He completes the purchase of his new main home on 1 September. He completes the sale of the buy-to-let flat on 3 September — two days later. His solicitor advises that because the buy-to-let was sold within days of the new purchase, the 3% SDLT surcharge does not apply to the new home.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: The 3% surcharge does not apply if the buyer sells their existing property around the same time as buying the new one — a near-simultaneous sale means the surcharge is avoided.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: The 3% surcharge is assessed at the end of the day of completion of the new purchase. On 1 September, George owned two residential properties (the new home + the buy-to-let). The surcharge therefore applies. However, because George sells the buy-to-let within 3 years of the new purchase, he can claim a refund of the 3% surcharge from HMRC — but the surcharge is still payable upfront at completion.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The surcharge is triggered at the end of the day of the new purchase — not by reference to what happens in the days following. George needed to have sold the buy-to-let on or before 1 September (the day of the new purchase) to avoid the surcharge arising. Selling two days later is too late. The refund mechanism exists but it is a subsequent recovery — the tax is due at the point of completion.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: The 3% SDLT surcharge applies if the buyer owns another residential property at the end of the day of the new purchase. Selling the old property even one day after the new purchase triggers the surcharge — though a refund can be claimed if the old property is sold within 3 years.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: SDLT 3% SURCHARGE · ADDITIONAL DWELLING · PROP TRAP — DISPOSAL WINDOW IS 3 YEARS · REPLACEMENT MAIN RESIDENCE · ATED · THREE YEAR RULE

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Harbour Estates sells a commercial office building (with a sitting tenant) to Apex Investments. Harbour has opted to tax. Apex's solicitor assumes that because there is a sitting tenant, the transaction automatically qualifies as a Transfer of a Going Concern (TOGC) and no VAT is payable. Apex does not opt to tax before completion.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: TOGC applies automatically whenever a commercial property with a sitting tenant is sold — the presence of a sitting tenant is enough to remove the transaction from the scope of VAT.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: TOGC has two conditions, both of which must be satisfied: (1) the property must be sold as a going concern (sitting tenant — satisfied here); AND (2) the buyer must opt to tax the property and notify HMRC before completion. If the buyer fails to opt to tax before completion, TOGC does not apply and the seller must charge VAT at 20% on the sale price.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario presents the sitting tenant condition as the only requirement, ignoring the buyer's obligation to opt to tax before completion. This is the most commonly failed condition in practice. Because Apex did not opt to tax, TOGC fails — Harbour must charge 20% VAT. Apex will receive a VAT invoice after completion and must pay the VAT. If Apex is VAT registered, they can recover it — but the cashflow impact and the risk of error make this a significant practical problem.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: TOGC requires the buyer to opt to tax AND notify HMRC before completion — not just a sitting tenant. Both conditions must be met. Always check the buyer's VAT position before exchange on a commercial investment property sale.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: TOGC · TRANSFER OF GOING CONCERN · PROP TRAP — BUYER MUST OPT TO TAX BEFORE COMPLETION · NOT AUTOMATIC · VAT ACT 1994 · NOTIFICATION REQUIRED

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A bank takes a mortgage over a property registered in the husband's sole name. Before completing the mortgage, the bank sends a surveyor to value the property. The wife is in hospital at the time and is not present during the valuation visit. The bank concludes that nobody else is in occupation and proceeds without making further enquiries. The wife later claims an overriding interest.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: A person who is temporarily absent from a property at the time of a visit is not 'in actual occupation' and therefore cannot have an overriding interest binding on a purchaser or mortgagee.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Actual occupation is not defeated by a temporary absence where the person intends to return and their belongings remain at the property (Chhokar v Chhokar [1984]). The wife's absence in hospital is temporary — she has not abandoned occupation. Her furniture and personal effects remain at the property. She is in actual occupation for the purposes of Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the intuition that 'occupation' requires physical presence at the moment of inspection. Courts have consistently rejected this narrow reading. A single visit that finds no one home does not establish absence of occupation — the bank needed to make enquiries of the registered proprietor about whether anyone else had an interest in the property, and should have sought written confirmation from the wife that she had no interest.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Actual occupation is not defeated by temporary absence — belongings remaining and an intention to return suffice. A bank (or any purchaser) must make enquiries of all apparent occupiers and cannot rely on absence at a single visit.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: ACTUAL OCCUPATION · OVERRIDING INTEREST · PROP TRAP — TEMPORARY ABSENCE DOES NOT DEFEAT OCCUPATION · CHHOKAR · BELONGINGS SUFFICIENT · LRA 2002 SCH 3 PARA 2

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A commercial landlord discovers on Monday that the tenant has been unlawfully subletting, in breach of the alienation covenant. On Friday of the same week, the landlord's accounts team processes and sends a demand for the quarterly rent due. The landlord's solicitor then serves a s.146 notice the following Monday, intending to forfeit.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Waiver requires a deliberate decision by the landlord to overlook a breach — a routine administrative rent demand sent without the landlord personally authorising it cannot constitute waiver.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Waiver is automatic and objective — it does not require deliberate intent. Any unequivocal act recognising the continued existence of the tenancy, done with knowledge of the breach, constitutes waiver. A demand for rent is a classic example, regardless of whether it was sent by an accounts team without specific authorisation from the landlord's decision-makers.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the assumption that waiver requires a knowing, deliberate choice. The law does not distinguish between a deliberate decision to accept rent and an administrative demand sent 'automatically'. Once the landlord (or their agent) had knowledge of the breach, any act recognising the tenancy — including a rent demand — waives the right to forfeit for that breach. The s.146 notice is now too late for this breach. The landlord must wait for a fresh breach.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Waiver is automatic and objective — any act recognising the tenancy (including a rent demand) after knowledge of the breach waives the right to forfeit for that breach, regardless of intent. On discovery of a breach, suspend all rent demands immediately.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: WAIVER OF FORFEITURE · AUTOMATIC · OBJECTIVE · PROP TRAP — NO DELIBERATE ELECTION REQUIRED · DEMAND FOR RENT · RECOGNITION OF TENANCY · LEGAL COSTS LETTER

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A commercial tenant has a 10-year lease with a break clause at year 5, exercisable on 6 months' written notice, conditional on all rent being paid to date. The tenant serves a break notice and believes the break is effective. However, there is an outstanding unpaid invoice of £50 relating to interest on a late rent payment — an amount the tenant did not even know was owed.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: A £50 shortfall is so trivial that the court will apply a de minimis principle and treat the break condition as satisfied — the landlord cannot rely on a minor technical arrear to defeat the break.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Break clause conditions requiring rent to be paid 'to date' are construed strictly. Courts have consistently held that even minor arrears — however small — defeat a break notice if the condition is not satisfied at the relevant date. There is no de minimis exception for break conditions in English law. The break fails and the lease continues.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario deliberately makes the arrear trivial (£50) to trigger the instinct that the law would not be so harsh. But in cases such as Avocet Industrial Estates LLP v Merol Ltd [2011] and Riverside Park Ltd v NHS Property Services Ltd [2016], courts have enforced break conditions literally regardless of the size of the shortfall. The condition is a binary test — either satisfied or not. The tenant's ignorance of the arrear is irrelevant.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Break conditions requiring rent to be paid to date are construed strictly — there is no de minimis exception. Even a trivial arrear defeats a break notice. Tenant solicitors must audit the rent account meticulously before serving a break notice and ensure every penny is paid.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: BREAK CLAUSE · STRICT COMPLIANCE · PROP TRAP — NO DE MINIMIS ON BREAK CONDITIONS · RENT MUST BE PAID IN FULL · PCE INVESTORS · CLEAN HANDS

Property Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Jamila buys a property using a mortgage. Her solicitor argues that there is a brief moment between the transfer being executed and the mortgage being executed — a 'scintilla temporis' — during which Jamila holds the freehold free of the mortgage. Jamila's friend, who contributed to the purchase price and lives at the property, claims her equitable interest arose during that moment and therefore ranks ahead of the mortgage.
Property Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: There is a scintilla temporis — a tiny moment of time — between the transfer of the legal estate to the buyer and the simultaneous creation of the mortgage, during which the buyer holds the property free of the charge. Any equitable interest arising in that moment ranks ahead of the mortgage.
Property Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: The doctrine of the scintilla temporis was rejected by the House of Lords in Abbey National Building Society v Cann [1991]. A purchase and a contemporaneous mortgage are treated as one indivisible transaction — there is no gap in time between them. The mortgage takes effect at the same instant as the transfer. No equitable interest can arise in the non-existent interval.
Property Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the logical appeal of the scintilla temporis argument — if the buyer becomes owner for even a moment before the mortgage is created, earlier interests logically rank higher. But Cann specifically rejected this reasoning: treating the purchase and mortgage as simultaneous prevents third parties from exploiting a manufactured gap to defeat lenders' security. The friend's equitable interest, if it exists, arises from the purchase money contribution — but it cannot gain priority over the mortgage by reference to a moment of time that the law treats as non-existent.
Property Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: There is no scintilla temporis between a purchase and a contemporaneous mortgage — Abbey National v Cann [1991]. The transfer and mortgage are one transaction. No equitable interest can arise in the gap because there is no gap.
Property Practice Traps.summary_line: SCINTILLA TEMPORIS · ABBEY NATIONAL V CANN · PROP TRAP — NO GAP BETWEEN PURCHASE AND MORTGAGE · SIMULTANEOUS TRANSACTIONS · OVERRIDING INTEREST CANNOT ARISE
